maximize E[R(x, y)] - beta * KL(pi_theta || pi_ref) where: R(x, y) = reward model score pi_theta = current policy (being trained) pi_ref = reference policy (frozen SFT model) beta = KL penalty coefficient

The coefficient beta controls the trade-off: larger beta keeps the model closer to the reference but limits improvement; smaller beta allows more divergence but risks reward hacking.

Key Insight

RLHF requires training four models simultaneously: the policy, the reference policy, the reward model, and the critic. This enormous memory footprint is one of the main motivations for developing alternatives like GRPO.

Section 5.3

Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)

PPO works well but is extremely expensive. It requires a separate critic (value) model — often as large as the policy model itself — effectively doubling the GPU memory requirement. GRPO, introduced by DeepSeek, elegantly eliminates this critic entirely.

5.3.1 The Problem with PPO: Memory Cost

Figure 5.7 — GRPO eliminates the critic model, saving significant GPU memory.

5.3.2 The GRPO Idea: Use the Group as Baseline

Instead of training a critic to estimate the baseline (expected reward), GRPO generates a group of responses for each prompt and uses the group's statistics as the baseline. The key insight: if you have enough samples, the mean reward of the group is an unbiased estimate of the expected reward.

Figure 5.8 — GRPO: generate a group of responses, score them, compute relative advantages, and update.

5.3.3 Group Sampling

For each prompt x in the training batch, GRPO samples G responses {y_1, y_2, ..., y_G} from the current policy. Each response is scored by a reward function r(x, y_i). This reward can come from a learned reward model, or from rule-based criteria (e.g., "is the final answer correct?").

The beauty of GRPO is that the reward function can be very simple. For math problems, it might just check whether the answer matches the ground truth. No expensive reward model needed.

5.3.4 Advantage Computation

The advantage of each response is computed by normalizing within the group:

GRPO Advantage
A_i = (r_i - mean(r_1, ..., r_G)) / std(r_1, ..., r_G) where: r_i = reward for response i G = group size (e.g., 8, 16, or 64)

This is strikingly simple. Responses better than the group average get positive advantage; worse ones get negative advantage. No critic needed — the group is the baseline.

5.3.5 GRPO Loss Function

The GRPO loss combines a clipped policy ratio (like PPO) with a KL divergence penalty:

GRPO Loss
L_GRPO = -E[ min( ratio_i * A_i, clip(ratio_i, 1-eps, 1+eps) * A_i ) ] + beta * KL(pi_theta || pi_ref) where: ratio_i = pi_theta(y_i|x) / pi_old(y_i|x) eps = clipping parameter (e.g., 0.2) beta = KL penalty weight

The clipping prevents overly large policy updates, just like in PPO. The KL penalty keeps the model from drifting too far from the reference policy.

5.3.6 PPO vs. GRPO: A Comparison

Aspect PPO GRPO
Baseline Learned critic (value network) Group mean reward
Models in memory 4 (policy, ref, critic, RM) 2-3 (policy, ref, optional RM)
Memory overhead Very high Moderate
Reward source Learned reward model Any function (can be rule-based)
Variance Low (critic reduces variance) Moderate (group averaging)
Simplicity Complex to implement Much simpler
Stability Sensitive to hyperparameters Relatively robust
Used by OpenAI (InstructGPT, ChatGPT) DeepSeek (R1, V3)
Key Insight

GRPO trades statistical efficiency for computational efficiency. A learned critic provides a lower-variance baseline, but the memory savings from eliminating it are enormous — often making the difference between training on a given cluster or not.

Section 5.4

Test-Time Compute & Search

So far we have focused on improving models during training. But there is another powerful lever: spending more compute at inference time. Rather than generating a single response, we can generate many candidates and select the best one, or build a search tree of reasoning paths.

5.4.1 The Test-Time Compute Paradigm

The idea is simple but profound: a smaller model that thinks longer can outperform a larger model that answers immediately. This mirrors human cognition — we solve hard problems by thinking more, not by growing bigger brains.

Figure 5.9 — Test-time compute: a smaller model with search can rival a much larger model.

5.4.2 Beam Search over Reasoning

Beam search maintains a set of the top-K most promising partial solutions and expands them at each step. Applied to reasoning, each "step" is a reasoning step or sentence, and we use a process reward model (PRM) to score each intermediate state.

Figure 5.10 — Beam search over reasoning steps: low-scoring branches are pruned at each level.

5.4.3 Best-of-N Sampling

The simplest test-time compute method is Best-of-N: generate N complete responses, score each one with a reward model (or verifier), and return the highest-scoring response. This is embarrassingly parallel and highly effective.

Figure 5.11 — Best-of-N sampling: generate many, verify, return the best.

5.4.4 Tree of Thought

Tree of Thought (ToT) extends chain-of-thought by allowing the model to explore multiple reasoning paths simultaneously and backtrack when a path seems unpromising. Instead of a single chain, we build a tree where each node is a reasoning state.

Figure 5.12 — Tree of Thought: branching reasoning paths with evaluation and pruning.

5.4.5 Compute Scaling at Inference

A key empirical finding is that test-time compute scales predictably: more search budget generally leads to better answers, following a smooth curve with diminishing returns.

Figure 5.13 — Compute scaling: accuracy improves with more test-time compute, with diminishing returns.

5.4.6 Train-Time vs. Test-Time Compute

There is a fundamental trade-off between investing compute at training time versus at inference time:

Dimension Train-Time Compute Test-Time Compute
When spent Once, during training Every query, during inference
Amortized cost Spread over all queries Per-query cost
Flexibility Fixed capability Adaptable per problem difficulty
Scaling law Chinchilla-style power law Smooth log-linear improvement
Best for General capability Hard, specific problems
Key Insight

The optimal strategy is to combine both: train a strong model, then let it search at inference time for hard problems. This is exactly what reasoning models like DeepSeek-R1 and OpenAI's o1 do.

Section 5.5

The Full Reasoning Model: R1-Style Training

We now have all the pieces to understand how a full reasoning model is built. DeepSeek-R1 demonstrated that open-source models can rival proprietary reasoning models by combining GRPO, chain-of-thought training, and a carefully staged pipeline.

5.5.1 The DeepSeek-R1 Recipe: Four Stages

Figure 5.14 — The four-stage DeepSeek-R1 training pipeline.

5.5.2 Cold Start: Distillation from a Stronger Model

The training process begins with a cold start. The base model does not know how to produce long, structured reasoning chains. To bootstrap this capability, we collect thousands of chain-of-thought examples from a stronger model (e.g., GPT-4 or DeepSeek-V3) and fine-tune on them.

This cold-start SFT data includes problems from mathematics, coding, and logical reasoning, each paired with detailed step-by-step solutions. The fine-tuned model learns the format of reasoning: how to break problems into steps, how to verify intermediate results, and how to present a final answer.

Importantly, the cold-start model is not yet a strong reasoner — it has learned the shape of reasoning but not the substance. The RL stage that follows will improve the actual quality of reasoning.

5.5.3 RL Training with GRPO and Rule-Based Rewards

The second stage is where the real improvement happens. We train the cold-start model with GRPO using rule-based rewards rather than a learned reward model. For math problems, the reward is simply:

Rule-Based Reward (Math)
r(x, y) = 1.0 if extract_answer(y) == ground_truth(x) r(x, y) = 0.0 otherwise Optional format reward: r_format(y) = 1.0 if y contains <think>...</think> tags r_format(y) = 0.0 otherwise

This is remarkably simple — and that is the beauty of it. No expensive reward model training is needed. The model learns to reason better simply by being rewarded for getting correct answers.

During RL training, a fascinating phenomenon emerges: the model spontaneously develops longer and more sophisticated reasoning chains. It learns to self-verify, reconsider, and correct mistakes — all without being explicitly taught to do so.

5.5.4 Thinking Tokens

R1-style models use special tokens to separate the reasoning process from the final answer. The model generates its thinking inside <think> tags, followed by a clean answer.

Example: R1-Style Reasoning Output 
<think>
The user asks: what is 27 * 43?

Let me break this down:
27 * 43 = 27 * 40 + 27 * 3
27 * 40 = 1080
27 * 3 = 81
1080 + 81 = 1161

Let me verify: 1161 / 27 = 43. Yes, correct.
</think>

27 multiplied by 43 equals 1,161.

The thinking tokens are typically hidden from the end user, but they are critical for the model's performance. They provide the "scratch paper" that allows explicit multi-step reasoning.

A key observation: the length of thinking grows during RL training. Early in training, the model produces short chains. As training progresses, the model learns that longer, more careful reasoning leads to higher rewards, and it naturally extends its chains — sometimes to thousands of tokens.

Example: Extended Reasoning with Self-Correction 
<think>
The problem asks: A farmer has 3 fields. Each field has
4 rows of crops. Each row has 8 plants. A storm destroys
25% of all plants. How many survive?

Step 1: Total plants = 3 * 4 * 8 = 96
Step 2: Plants destroyed = 96 * 0.25 = 24
Step 3: Plants surviving = 96 - 24 = 72

Wait, let me double-check step 1.
3 fields * 4 rows = 12 rows total.
12 rows * 8 plants = 96 plants. Correct.

And 25% of 96: 96/4 = 24. Correct.
96 - 24 = 72.

I am confident the answer is 72.
</think>

72 plants survive the storm.

Notice the self-verification ("Wait, let me double-check..."). This behavior emerges from RL training — it was never explicitly programmed. The model discovers that double-checking leads to more correct answers, which leads to higher reward.

5.5.5 The Full Pipeline

Putting it all together, the complete R1-style pipeline flows through multiple rounds of SFT and RL:

Figure 5.15 — The complete R1 pipeline: Base Model → SFT → RL → Rejection Sampling → SFT → RL → R1.

Each stage serves a distinct purpose:

  1. Cold-start SFT: Teaches the model the format of reasoning (how to use thinking tokens, how to structure steps).
  2. First RL (GRPO): Improves reasoning quality on domains with verifiable answers (math, code).
  3. Rejection sampling + data curation: Uses the RL-trained model to generate high-quality reasoning data. Only correct responses are kept. General-purpose SFT data (writing, QA, summarization) is mixed in.
  4. Second SFT: Trains on the curated mixture, restoring general capabilities while preserving reasoning.
  5. Second RL: Final alignment round, including safety, helpfulness, and reasoning rewards.

5.5.6 Results and Benchmarks

The staged training pipeline produces models with remarkable reasoning capabilities. Below are representative benchmark results showing how R1-style models compare.

Benchmark GPT-4o Claude 3.5 Sonnet DeepSeek-R1 OpenAI o1
AIME 2024 (math) 9.3% 16.0% 79.8% 79.2%
MATH-500 74.6% 78.3% 97.3% 96.4%
Codeforces (code) 23.0% 20.3% 96.3% 96.6%
GPQA Diamond (science) 49.9% 65.0% 71.5% 75.7%
MMLU (general) 87.2% 88.3% 90.8% 91.8%

The most striking results are in math competitions (AIME) and competitive programming (Codeforces), where reasoning models dramatically outperform standard LLMs. This is exactly where chain-of-thought reasoning and test-time compute provide the greatest advantage.

Key Insight

DeepSeek-R1 showed that the entire reasoning model pipeline can be built with open-source components and rule-based rewards. No proprietary reward model, no expensive human preference data for reasoning — just "is the answer correct?" as the reward signal, combined with GRPO. This dramatically lowers the barrier to building reasoning models.

5.5.7 Emergent Behaviors

Perhaps the most fascinating aspect of R1-style training is the emergent behaviors that arise during RL. Without explicit instruction, the model learns to:

  • Self-verify: "Let me check that result..." appears naturally in reasoning chains.
  • Backtrack: "Wait, that approach doesn't work. Let me try another way."
  • Explore alternatives: "There are two ways to solve this. Method 1: ..."
  • Allocate compute adaptively: Simple questions get short chains; hard questions get long chains.
  • Use multiple representations: The model switches between algebraic, geometric, and numerical reasoning as needed.

These behaviors emerge because they lead to more correct answers, and more correct answers lead to higher reward. The RL objective does not prescribe how to reason — only that the final answer should be correct. The model discovers effective reasoning strategies on its own.

5.5.8 Distillation: Smaller Reasoning Models

A final important result from DeepSeek-R1: the reasoning capability of a large model can be distilled into much smaller models. By fine-tuning a 7B or 14B parameter model on the reasoning traces generated by the full R1 model, we obtain smaller models that retain much of the reasoning ability.

Model Parameters AIME 2024 MATH-500
DeepSeek-R1 671B (MoE) 79.8% 97.3%
R1-Distill-Qwen-32B 32B 72.6% 94.3%
R1-Distill-Qwen-14B 14B 69.7% 93.9%
R1-Distill-Qwen-7B 7B 55.5% 92.8%
R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B 1.5B 28.6% 83.9%

Even a 1.5B parameter model, when distilled from R1's reasoning traces, achieves 83.9% on MATH-500 — far exceeding what a standard 1.5B model can achieve. This suggests that reasoning capability, once discovered by a large model, can be efficiently transferred to smaller ones.

Chapter Summary

In this chapter we traced the complete path from simple prompting tricks to full-fledged reasoning models:

  1. Chain-of-Thought showed that explicit intermediate steps dramatically improve reasoning, but it only elicits existing capability.
  2. RLHF provided the framework for training models to produce better outputs, using human preferences and reward models.
  3. GRPO simplified the RL pipeline by eliminating the critic model and using group-relative advantages, making reasoning model training practical.
  4. Test-time compute showed that spending more compute at inference — through search, sampling, and verification — can substitute for model size.
  5. R1-style training combined all these ideas into a multi-stage pipeline that produces models with emergent reasoning capabilities.

The key lesson: reasoning in LLMs is not magic. It is the product of careful training methodology — giving models scratch paper (thinking tokens), rewarding correct answers (GRPO with rule-based rewards), and iterating through multiple rounds of SFT and RL. The result is models that can solve problems previously thought to require human-level intelligence.

Chapter 6

Modern LLM Architectures

From GPT to Mamba: understanding the building blocks, design choices, and tradeoffs behind today's most powerful language models.

Section 6.1

GPT Architecture Deep Dive

The GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) family pioneered the decoder-only transformer paradigm. Rather than using the full encoder-decoder stack from the original "Attention Is All You Need" paper, GPT strips away the encoder entirely and relies solely on masked self-attention to generate text autoregressively — one token at a time, left to right.

Decoder-Only vs. Encoder-Decoder

Why did OpenAI choose a decoder-only design? The answer lies in simplicity and scalability. An encoder-decoder model (like T5 or BART) processes input through one stack and generates output through another, with cross-attention connecting them. A decoder-only model treats both input and output as a single sequence, simplifying training and inference.

Figure 6.1 — Encoder-Decoder vs. Decoder-Only architecture. GPT removes the encoder and cross-attention, using a single unified stack.

The decoder-only approach offers several advantages: (1) a single, uniform stack is simpler to scale, (2) the same architecture handles both "understanding" and "generation," and (3) in-context learning emerges naturally from the autoregressive formulation — you simply prepend examples as part of the input sequence.

The Causal Mask

The defining feature of the GPT decoder is the causal attention mask. During self-attention, each token can only attend to itself and all previous tokens — never to future tokens. This is enforced by a triangular mask matrix applied to the attention scores before the softmax.

Figure 6.2 — The lower-triangular causal mask. Blue cells are visible; gray cells are masked to negative infinity before softmax.

The mask ensures that when predicting "sat", the model can see "The" and "cat" but not "on" or "the." This is what makes generation autoregressive — the model produces one token at a time, each conditioned only on the past.

Full GPT Pipeline

Below is the complete data flow through a GPT model, from raw text input to predicted next-token probabilities.

Figure 6.3 — Complete GPT pipeline: text in, probability distribution out.

Inside the Transformer Block

Each transformer block in GPT consists of two sub-layers, each wrapped with a residual connection and layer normalization. GPT-2 introduced pre-norm (LayerNorm before the sub-layer rather than after), which improves training stability.

Figure 6.4 — A single GPT-2 transformer block with pre-norm residual connections.

GPT Model Family Comparison

Property GPT-2 GPT-3 GPT-4
Parameters1.5B175B~1.8T (rumored MoE)
Layers4896~120 (estimated)
Hidden dim160012288Not disclosed
Heads2596Not disclosed
Context length102420488K / 32K / 128K
Training data40 GB (WebText)570 GB filtered~13T tokens (est.)
NormalizationPre-LayerNormPre-LayerNormRMSNorm (likely)
ActivationGELUGELUSwiGLU (likely)
Position encodingLearned absoluteLearned absoluteUnknown
Year201920202023
Key Insight

GPT's core innovation wasn't architectural novelty — it was the insight that a simple decoder-only transformer, trained with enough data and compute, could exhibit emergent abilities like in-context learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, and code generation.

Section 6.2

LLaMA & Open-Source LLMs

Meta's LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) demonstrated that smaller, well-trained models can match or exceed larger ones. LLaMA introduced several architectural refinements that have become standard in open-source LLMs: RMSNorm, RoPE, SwiGLU, and Grouped Query Attention.

RMSNorm: Simpler Normalization

Standard LayerNorm computes both mean and variance, then normalizes and applies learned affine parameters. RMSNorm (Root Mean Square Normalization) drops the mean-centering step, using only the root mean square for normalization. This saves computation while maintaining performance.

LayerNorm:
y = (x - mean(x)) / sqrt(var(x) + eps) * gamma + beta

RMSNorm:
y = x / sqrt(mean(x^2) + eps) * gamma

No mean-centering, no beta bias term. Fewer ops, same quality.
PropertyLayerNormRMSNorm
Mean centeringYesNo
Variance normalizationYesRMS only
Learnable bias (beta)YesNo
Learnable scale (gamma)YesYes
Computation costHigher~33% fewer ops
Used inGPT-2/3, BERTLLaMA, Gemma, Mistral

RoPE: Rotary Position Embeddings

Instead of adding absolute position embeddings to token vectors, Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) encode position by rotating query and key vectors in 2D subspaces. The angle of rotation depends on the position and the dimension index, making relative positions naturally emerge through the dot product of rotated vectors.

Figure 6.5 — RoPE rotates each 2D pair of dimensions by a position-dependent angle. Relative position information is preserved through dot products.

SwiGLU Activation

LLaMA replaces the standard GELU activation in the feed-forward network with SwiGLU, a gated linear unit variant. SwiGLU combines the Swish activation (x * sigmoid(x)) with a gating mechanism, providing smoother gradients and better performance.

Standard FFN (GELU):
FFN(x) = GELU(x * W1 + b1) * W2 + b2

SwiGLU FFN:
FFN(x) = (Swish(x * W_gate) . (x * W_up)) * W_down

where Swish(x) = x * sigmoid(beta * x)
Note: SwiGLU uses 3 weight matrices instead of 2, so d_ff is reduced to 2/3 * 4d to match parameter count.

Grouped Query Attention (GQA)

Standard multi-head attention (MHA) gives each head its own Q, K, and V projections. This is expensive during inference because every K and V must be stored in the KV-cache. Grouped Query Attention shares K and V heads across groups of query heads, dramatically reducing KV-cache size while retaining most of MHA's quality.

Figure 6.6 — Multi-Head Attention vs. Multi-Query Attention vs. Grouped Query Attention. GQA strikes the optimal balance between quality and inference speed.

LLaMA Architecture Summary

Figure 6.7 — LLaMA architecture: RMSNorm, RoPE, GQA, and SwiGLU form the modern open-source LLM blueprint.
Impact

LLaMA's architectural choices — RMSNorm, RoPE, SwiGLU, GQA — have been adopted by virtually every major open-source LLM: Mistral, Qwen, Gemma, Yi, DeepSeek, and many others. This blueprint is now the de facto standard for efficient transformer-based language models.

Section 6.3

Mixture of Experts (MoE)

Dense models activate all parameters for every token. Mixture of Experts (MoE) architectures activate only a subset of parameters per token, enabling massive model capacity with manageable computation costs. This is the core idea behind sparse models like Mixtral, Switch Transformer, and (reportedly) GPT-4.

Dense vs. Sparse Models

In a dense model, every neuron in every layer fires for every input. In a sparse MoE model, a router selects a small number of expert sub-networks (typically 2 out of 8) for each token, leaving the rest dormant.

Figure 6.8 — Dense models activate all parameters; MoE models activate only selected expert sub-networks per token.

Router / Gating Mechanism

The router (or gating network) is a small linear layer that takes each token's hidden state and produces a probability distribution over the available experts. The top-K experts (usually K=2) are selected, and their outputs are weighted by the router's softmax probabilities.

Figure 6.9 — The router scores all experts, selects the top-K, renormalizes weights, and combines their outputs.

Load Balancing

A naive router often converges to sending most tokens to a few "popular" experts while others remain unused. This expert collapse wastes capacity. Load balancing losses penalize uneven distribution, encouraging all experts to receive roughly equal traffic.

Auxiliary Load Balancing Loss:
L_balance = alpha * N * sum_i(f_i * P_i)

where f_i = fraction of tokens routed to expert i
P_i = average router probability for expert i
alpha = balancing coefficient (typically 0.01)
N = number of experts

Capacity Factor and Token Overflow

Each expert has a fixed capacity — the maximum number of tokens it can process per batch. The capacity factor (CF) controls this: capacity = CF * (total_tokens / num_experts). If CF=1.0, each expert can handle exactly its fair share. Tokens exceeding capacity are either dropped or routed to a fallback.

  • CF < 1.0: Some tokens will be dropped (faster but lossy)
  • CF = 1.0: Exact balance, no slack
  • CF > 1.0: Buffer for imbalance (e.g., 1.25 allows 25% overflow)

Switch Transformer vs. Mixtral

PropertySwitch TransformerMixtral 8x7B
Top-K experts1 (single expert)2 (top-2)
Number of expertsUp to 20488
Expert typeFFN replacementFFN replacement
Total parameters1.6T (largest)46.7B
Active parameters~1/2048 per token~12.9B per token
Load balancingAuxiliary loss + dropoutAuxiliary loss
Capacity factorExplicit (1.0-1.5)No capacity limit
Architecture baseT5 (encoder-decoder)Mistral (decoder-only)
Performance4x speedup over T5Matches LLaMA 2 70B
Key Insight

MoE decouples total parameters (model capacity, knowledge) from active parameters (compute per token). Mixtral 8x7B has 46.7B total parameters but only uses ~12.9B per token — achieving 70B-class quality at 13B-class speed.

Section 6.4

State Space Models (Mamba)

Transformers' self-attention has a fundamental limitation: it scales quadratically with sequence length — O(n²) in both time and memory. State Space Models (SSMs), and Mamba in particular, offer a linear-time alternative that can process very long sequences efficiently while maintaining competitive performance.

Attention's O(n²) Bottleneck

In standard attention, every token attends to every other token, producing an n x n attention matrix. This means doubling the sequence length quadruples the compute. At 100K+ tokens, this becomes prohibitively expensive.

Figure 6.10 — Compute scaling with sequence length. Attention's quadratic growth becomes untenable for long sequences.

SSM Basics: Continuous-to-Discrete State Space

A State Space Model maps an input sequence x(t) to an output y(t) through a latent state h(t). The continuous-time formulation is discretized for digital computation using a step size parameter delta.

Continuous SSM:
h'(t) = A * h(t) + B * x(t)
y(t) = C * h(t) + D * x(t)

Discretized SSM (using zero-order hold):
h_k = A_bar * h_{k-1} + B_bar * x_k
y_k = C * h_k

where A_bar = exp(delta * A)
B_bar = (delta * A)^{-1} * (exp(delta * A) - I) * delta * B

A is the state transition matrix (N x N), B is input matrix (N x 1), C is output matrix (1 x N), delta is the discretization step.

The key insight of prior SSM work (S4, H3) was finding ways to initialize and parameterize the A matrix so that it can capture long-range dependencies — using structured matrices like the HiPPO initialization that compress history into a fixed-size state.

Selective Scan: Mamba's Key Innovation

Prior SSMs used fixed (input-independent) parameters A, B, C, delta. Mamba's breakthrough is making B, C, and delta input-dependent — computed dynamically from the input sequence. This gives the model the ability to selectively focus on or ignore information based on content, similar to how attention gates information, but with linear complexity.

Figure 6.11 — Mamba's selective scan: B, C, and delta are computed from input, enabling content-aware state transitions with linear complexity.

Mamba Block Architecture

Figure 6.12 — A single Mamba block: dual-branch with Conv1D, selective SSM, and gated output.

Attention vs. SSM Tradeoffs

PropertyTransformer (Attention)Mamba (SSM)
Sequence complexityO(n^2)O(n)
Memory for contextO(n) KV-cacheO(1) fixed state
Training parallelismFully parallelParallel scan
InferenceAuto-regressive + KV-cacheRecurrent (very fast)
Long-range retrievalExcellent (direct access)Good (compressed in state)
In-context learningStrongEmerging
Hardware efficiencyGood (FlashAttention)Excellent (less memory)
MaturityHighly matureRapidly developing
Key Insight

Mamba achieves 5x throughput on long sequences compared to transformers of similar size, with constant memory per token during inference. However, its fixed-size state means it must learn to compress information, whereas attention can directly retrieve any past token. Hybrid architectures (like Jamba) combine both approaches.

Section 6.5

Multimodal Architectures

Modern LLMs are increasingly multimodal — they process not just text but also images, video, and audio. The central challenge is bridging different modality encoders with a unified language model. This section explores the key architectural patterns for building vision-language models.

Vision Encoder: ViT

The Vision Transformer (ViT) converts images into sequences of tokens, making them compatible with transformer-based language models. An image is split into fixed-size patches (typically 14x14 or 16x16 pixels), each linearly projected into a token embedding, then processed by a standard transformer encoder.

Figure 6.13 — ViT splits an image into patches, projects each to a token, adds positional encodings, and processes them through transformer layers.

Projection / Adapter Layer

Vision tokens live in the vision encoder's embedding space, while the LLM expects tokens in its own space. A projection layer (or adapter) bridges this gap. This can be as simple as a linear layer or as complex as a small transformer (Q-Former in BLIP-2, Perceiver resampler in Flamingo).

Cross-Attention vs. Late Fusion

Figure 6.14 — Cross-attention weaves visual information into every layer; late fusion simply prepends projected visual tokens as a prefix.

LLaVA Architecture

LLaVA (Large Language-and-Vision Assistant) popularized the simple but effective late-fusion approach: freeze a CLIP vision encoder, train a small MLP projector, then fine-tune the LLM to understand visual tokens.

Figure 6.15 — LLaVA: a frozen CLIP ViT encodes images, an MLP projects to LLM space, and visual + text tokens are concatenated as input to the language model.

Video and Audio Extensions

Extending to video involves sampling frames, encoding each with the vision encoder, then either concatenating all frame tokens (expensive) or using temporal pooling/attention to compress. For audio, models like Whisper produce audio token sequences that can be projected into the same LLM embedding space.

Figure 6.16 — Multi-stream architecture: each modality has its own encoder and projector; tokens are merged before the unified LLM.
Section 6.6

Diffusion Transformers (DiT)

Diffusion models have become the dominant paradigm for image (and increasingly video) generation. The Diffusion Transformer (DiT) replaces the traditional U-Net backbone with a transformer, yielding better scalability and quality. This architecture underpins models like DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion 3, and Sora.

The Diffusion Process

Diffusion models work in two phases: a forward process that gradually adds Gaussian noise to clean data over T timesteps until it becomes pure noise, and a reverse process where a neural network learns to denoise step by step, recovering the original data.

Figure 6.17 — Diffusion: gradually add noise (forward), then train a network to reverse the process, generating images from pure noise.

U-Net vs. Transformer Backbone

Traditional diffusion models (DDPM, Stable Diffusion 1.x/2.x) use a U-Net backbone with convolutional layers and skip connections. DiT replaces this entirely with a transformer, treating image patches as tokens — the same way ViT processes images.

Figure 6.18 — U-Net uses an encoder-decoder with skip connections; DiT uses uniform transformer blocks, scaling more predictably.

DiT Architecture with AdaLN

DiT conditions on the timestep t and optional class/text embeddings using Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN). Instead of standard LayerNorm with fixed learned scale and shift, AdaLN computes scale (gamma) and shift (beta) from the conditioning signal, allowing the model to modulate its behavior differently at each denoising step.

Figure 6.19 — DiT block: AdaLN modulates normalization parameters based on timestep and conditioning, with learnable alpha scaling for residuals.

Latent Diffusion

Operating diffusion in pixel space is extremely expensive (a 512x512 image has 786K dimensions). Latent diffusion first compresses the image via a pre-trained VAE encoder into a compact latent representation (e.g., 64x64x4), runs the diffusion process in this latent space, then decodes back to pixels.

Latent Diffusion Pipeline:
1. Encode: z_0 = Encoder(x)    [512x512x3 -> 64x64x4]
2. Forward: z_t = sqrt(alpha_t)*z_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_t)*eps
3. Reverse: z_0_hat = DiT(z_t, t, c)    [predict noise or z_0]
4. Decode: x_hat = Decoder(z_0_hat)    [64x64x4 -> 512x512x3]

Compression ratio: ~48x fewer dimensions. Massive compute savings.

Classifier-Free Guidance

Classifier-free guidance (CFG) improves sample quality by amplifying the difference between conditional and unconditional predictions. During training, the conditioning signal is randomly dropped some fraction of the time. At inference, the model runs twice — once with and once without conditioning — and the outputs are interpolated.

Classifier-Free Guidance:
eps_guided = eps_unconditional + w * (eps_conditional - eps_unconditional)

where w = guidance scale (typically 5-15)
w = 1.0: pure conditional (standard)
w > 1.0: amplified conditioning (higher quality, less diversity)
w = 0.0: unconditional generation
Section 6.7

Architecture Comparison Dashboard

With so many architectures available, choosing the right one for a given task requires understanding their tradeoffs across multiple dimensions: parameter efficiency, speed, memory, quality, and scaling behavior.

Parameter Efficiency Comparison

Architecture Total Params Active Params Efficiency Ratio
GPT-3 (Dense)175B175B1.0x
LLaMA 2 70B (Dense)70B70B1.0x
Mixtral 8x7B (MoE)46.7B12.9B3.6x
GPT-4 (rumored MoE)~1.8T~280B~6.4x
Switch-XXL (MoE)1.6T~1.5B~1000x
Mamba 2.8B (SSM)2.8B2.8B1.0x
Jamba (Hybrid)52B12B4.3x

Speed and Throughput Benchmarks

Architecture Training Speed Inference Latency Long-Context Throughput
Dense Transformer (7B)BaselineBaselineDegrades at >32K
GQA Transformer (7B)~1x~1.5x fasterBetter (less KV-cache)
MoE 8x7B~1.5x faster*~2x faster**Similar to dense
Mamba (3B)~1.2x~5x fasterLinear scaling
Hybrid (Jamba)~1.1x~3x fasterGood to 256K+

* Per active FLOP. ** With expert parallelism. All numbers approximate and workload-dependent.

Memory Footprint Comparison

Architecture Model Weights (FP16) KV-Cache (32K ctx) Total Inference Memory
LLaMA 2 7B (MHA)14 GB~2 GB~16 GB
LLaMA 2 7B (GQA)14 GB~0.5 GB~14.5 GB
Mixtral 8x7B93 GB~0.5 GB~93.5 GB
Mamba 3B6 GB~0 (fixed state)~6 GB
Jamba 52B104 GB~0.5 GB~104.5 GB
Note on MoE Memory

MoE models use fewer FLOPs per token but still require all expert weights to be loaded in memory. Mixtral 8x7B uses 12.9B FLOPs per token but needs 93 GB to store all 46.7B parameters. This is the MoE memory paradox: fast compute, heavy memory.

Quality Metrics

Model MMLU (5-shot) HumanEval GSM8K HellaSwag
GPT-3 175B43.9~0~578.9
LLaMA 2 70B68.929.956.885.3
Mixtral 8x7B70.640.274.486.7
LLaMA 3 70B79.548.283.088.0
GPT-486.467.092.095.3
Mamba 2.8B~35~15~20~65

Scaling Behavior

Figure 6.20 — Scaling laws show that loss decreases as a power law with compute. MoE architectures achieve better loss-per-FLOP; SSMs are competitive but still maturing.

When to Use What: Decision Guide

Scenario Recommended Architecture Reason
General-purpose chatbot Dense Transformer (LLaMA-style) Best quality, mature tooling
Maximum quality, any cost Large MoE GPT-4-class: MoE achieves best quality per training FLOP
Edge / mobile deployment Small Dense (1-3B) MoE memory overhead defeats the purpose at small scale
Very long documents (100K+) Mamba or Hybrid Linear complexity, constant KV memory
High-throughput serving MoE with expert parallelism More tokens/second per GPU at same quality
Image/video understanding LLaVA-style multimodal Proven pattern, many open-source options
Image/video generation DiT (latent diffusion) State-of-the-art quality, good scaling
Real-time streaming Mamba / SSM Fastest inference, O(1) state update
Research / experimentation Hybrid (Jamba-style) Best of both worlds, active research area
Chapter Summary

Modern LLM architectures span a rich design space. The dense transformer (GPT, LLaMA) remains the workhorse, refined with RMSNorm, RoPE, SwiGLU, and GQA. Mixture of Experts decouples capacity from compute, enabling massive models at manageable cost. State space models (Mamba) offer linear-time alternatives for long-context applications. Multimodal architectures extend LLMs to vision and beyond, while Diffusion Transformers bring transformer scaling to generative image/video models. The field is rapidly converging on hybrid approaches that combine the strengths of each paradigm.

End of Chapter 6

Next: Chapter 7 — Training Large Language Models

Chapter 7

RAG from Scratch

Retrieval-Augmented Generation: teaching language models to look things up before they answer.

Section 7.1

Vector Embeddings & Similarity

Before a language model can retrieve anything, it needs a way to understand meaning mathematically. This is the job of vector embeddings — numerical representations that capture the semantic essence of words, sentences, or entire documents.

Words as Numbers

An embedding model takes a piece of text and maps it to a fixed-length vector of floating-point numbers. Each dimension in this vector captures some latent aspect of meaning. The critical insight: similar meanings produce nearby vectors.

Figure 7.1 — A word passes through an embedding model and becomes a high-dimensional vector.

Modern embedding models like OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small or open-source all-MiniLM-L6-v2 produce vectors with hundreds or thousands of dimensions. Each dimension is not directly interpretable by humans, but collectively they encode rich semantic information.

2D Embedding Space

To visualize how embeddings work, imagine projecting words down to just two dimensions. Words with similar meanings naturally cluster together.

Figure 7.2 — Words projected into 2D space cluster by semantic category.

In reality, embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions, but the principle is the same: semantic proximity maps to geometric proximity. "Cat" is closer to "dog" than to "pizza" in embedding space.

Word Analogies: Vector Arithmetic

One of the most famous properties of well-trained embeddings is that they support vector arithmetic for analogies. The classic example:

Vector Analogy
vec("king") − vec("man") + vec("woman") ≈ vec("queen")
Figure 7.3 — The "gender" direction is consistent: subtracting it from king and adding it yields queen.

This works because the embedding space has learned consistent directional relationships. The vector from "man" to "woman" encodes a gender direction, and when applied to "king," it lands near "queen."

Cosine Similarity

How do we measure whether two vectors are "close"? The standard metric in RAG systems is cosine similarity, which measures the angle between two vectors regardless of their magnitude.

Cosine Similarity
cos(θ) = (A · B) / (||A|| × ||B||)
Figure 7.4 — Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors. Smaller angle = higher similarity.

Dot Product vs. Cosine Similarity

Two common similarity metrics are the dot product and cosine similarity. The key difference is how they handle vector magnitude:

Property Dot Product Cosine Similarity
Formula A · B = Σ aibi (A · B) / (||A|| ||B||)
Range (−∞, +∞) [−1, +1]
Magnitude-sensitive? Yes — longer vectors score higher No — direction only
Best when Magnitude encodes importance You want pure semantic angle
Common in RAG? Yes (after normalization) Yes (most common default)
Key Insight

When vectors are L2-normalized (unit length), dot product and cosine similarity are identical. Most embedding models output normalized vectors, so in practice the two metrics give the same rankings.

The Challenge of High Dimensions

Real embeddings live in 384, 768, or even 3072 dimensions. We can only visualize 2D or 3D, so all our scatter plots are projections — dimensionality reduction techniques like t-SNE or UMAP that preserve local structure while discarding most of the space.

Figure 7.5 — High-dimensional embeddings are projected into 2D for visualization, preserving local neighborhoods.

The important takeaway: even though we draw 2D diagrams, the real power of embeddings comes from having hundreds of dimensions to express nuanced relationships. Two words might appear close in a 2D projection but actually be distant in the full space, or vice versa.

Section 7.2

Vector Databases & Indexing

Once you have millions of document embeddings, you need a way to find the nearest neighbors to a query vector fast. Brute-force comparison is O(n) per query — too slow for production. This is where specialized indexing algorithms come in.

Why Vector Databases?

A traditional database indexes structured data with B-trees. A vector database indexes high-dimensional points and answers the question: "which stored vectors are most similar to this query vector?" It trades a small amount of accuracy for massive speed gains through Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search.

The Scale Problem

Comparing a 768-dim query against 10 million 768-dim vectors using brute force requires ~7.68 billion floating-point multiplications per query. ANN indexes reduce this to checking only a few thousand candidates.

HNSW: Hierarchical Navigable Small World

HNSW builds a multi-layer graph where each layer is progressively sparser. Search starts at the top (fewest nodes, longest jumps) and descends through layers, refining the search region at each level.

Figure 7.6 — HNSW search traverses layers top-down, taking long jumps in sparse layers and refining in dense layers.

HNSW achieves O(log n) query time and is the most popular ANN algorithm for vector databases. Its main trade-off is high memory usage since it stores the full graph in RAM.

IVF: Inverted File Index

IVF partitions the vector space into clusters using k-means. At query time, only vectors in the nearest clusters are searched, dramatically reducing the search space.

Figure 7.7 — IVF partitions space into Voronoi cells. The query (red) only searches the nearest cluster.

Product Quantization

Product Quantization (PQ) compresses vectors to reduce memory usage. It splits each vector into sub-vectors and replaces each sub-vector with the index of its nearest centroid from a learned codebook.

Figure 7.8 — Product Quantization replaces sub-vectors with codebook indices, achieving massive compression.

Index Comparison

Method Query Speed Recall@10 Memory Build Time
Brute Force Slow (O(n)) 100% (exact) High None
HNSW Very Fast (O(log n)) 95–99% Very High Moderate
IVF Fast 90–97% Medium Fast (k-means)
IVF + PQ Fast 85–95% Very Low Moderate

Popular Vector Databases

Database Type Index Algorithms Managed Cloud? Best For
Pinecone Managed SaaS Proprietary Yes Easiest setup, production scale
Weaviate Open-source HNSW Yes Hybrid search, modules
Chroma Open-source HNSW No Local dev, Python-native
FAISS Library IVF, PQ, HNSW No Research, custom pipelines
Qdrant Open-source HNSW Yes Filtering + search, Rust perf
Milvus Open-source IVF, HNSW, DiskANN Yes (Zilliz) Billion-scale, distributed
Choosing a Vector Database

For prototyping, Chroma or FAISS get you started in minutes. For production at scale with minimal ops burden, Pinecone or Qdrant Cloud are strong choices. For billion-scale datasets, consider Milvus.

Section 7.3

Chunking & Retrieval Strategies

RAG does not embed entire documents as single vectors. Documents are split into chunks — smaller segments that each become one embedding. The art of chunking has an outsized impact on retrieval quality.

Why Chunking Matters

The choice of chunk size creates a fundamental trade-off:

Figure 7.9 — Chunks that are too large dilute meaning; chunks too small lose context.

The sweet spot for most RAG applications is 200–500 tokens per chunk, roughly a paragraph or two. But the best size depends on your content and queries.

Fixed-Size Chunking

The simplest approach: split text into chunks of exactly N tokens, with optional overlap. Think of a sliding window moving across the document.

Figure 7.10 — Fixed-size chunking: a window of constant token length slides across the document.

Semantic Chunking

Instead of fixed sizes, semantic chunking finds natural topic boundaries. It uses embedding similarity between consecutive sentences: when similarity drops below a threshold, a new chunk begins.

Figure 7.11 — Semantic chunking detects topic shifts by monitoring inter-sentence similarity.

Overlap Strategies

Overlap between chunks ensures that information at chunk boundaries is not lost. A typical overlap is 10–20% of the chunk size.

Figure 7.12 — Overlapping chunks share content at boundaries (purple regions) to preserve context continuity.

The Retrieval Pipeline

Once documents are chunked and embedded, the retrieval pipeline responds to user queries through a multi-stage process:

Figure 7.13 — Full retrieval pipeline: embed the query, search the index, optionally rerank, return top chunks.
The Two-Stage Retrieve-Then-Rerank Pattern

A bi-encoder (the embedding model) quickly narrows millions of chunks down to ~20 candidates. Then a slower but more accurate cross-encoder reranker scores each candidate against the query jointly. This gives you both speed and precision.

Common retrieval parameters to tune:

  • Chunk size: 256–512 tokens for most use cases
  • Chunk overlap: 10–20% of chunk size
  • Top-K retrieval: 5–20 candidates before reranking
  • Final K: 3–5 chunks passed to the LLM
  • Similarity threshold: filter out low-confidence matches
Section 7.4

Generation with Retrieved Context

Retrieval alone is not enough — the retrieved chunks must be woven into a prompt that guides the language model to produce a grounded, accurate answer. This is where the "G" in RAG happens.

Context Stuffing: The Prompt Template

The simplest approach is context stuffing: concatenate all retrieved chunks into the system or user prompt, followed by the user's question.

Figure 7.14 — Context stuffing: retrieved chunks are injected into the prompt before the user's question.

Context Window Management

Every LLM has a finite context window — the maximum number of tokens it can process at once. You must budget this window carefully:

Figure 7.15 — Budget your context window: system instructions, retrieved context, query, and generation output all compete for space.

The "Lost in the Middle" Problem

Research has shown that LLMs pay more attention to content at the beginning and end of the context, while information in the middle gets less attention. This is the "lost in the middle" phenomenon.

Figure 7.16 — The "U-shaped" attention pattern: models attend most to the beginning and end of context.
Practical Implication

Place the most relevant chunks first and last in your context. Bury less critical supporting information in the middle. Some systems sort retrieved chunks by relevance in a "sandwich" pattern: best first, second-best last, rest in the middle.

Map-Reduce for Long Contexts

When you have more retrieved content than fits in a single context window, you can use a map-reduce strategy: process chunks in parallel, then combine the results.

Figure 7.17 — Map-reduce: process chunk groups in parallel (map), then combine intermediate results (reduce).

The map-reduce pattern is especially useful when:

  • You have far more relevant content than fits in one context window
  • The question requires synthesizing information from many sources
  • You want to parallelize LLM calls for speed

The trade-off is cost (multiple LLM calls) and potential information loss during the summarization step. An alternative is the refine pattern, where each chunk's answer iteratively refines the previous answer.

Section 7.5

Advanced RAG Patterns

Basic RAG — embed, retrieve, generate — works surprisingly well, but production systems need more sophisticated strategies to handle ambiguous queries, improve precision, and decide when retrieval is even necessary.

Query Rewriting & Expansion

User queries are often vague, misspelled, or use different terminology than the documents. Query rewriting uses the LLM to reformulate the query before embedding.

Figure 7.18 — Query rewriting transforms a vague question into multiple precise search queries.

HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings

HyDE flips the script: instead of embedding the query, ask the LLM to generate a hypothetical answer, then embed that. The intuition is that a fake-but-plausible answer is closer in embedding space to real answers than the question itself.

Figure 7.19 — HyDE: generate a plausible answer first, then use its embedding to retrieve real documents.

Multi-Hop Retrieval

Some questions require information from multiple documents that are not directly related to the query. Multi-hop retrieval performs iterative searches, using retrieved context to formulate new queries.

Figure 7.20 — Multi-hop retrieval: each hop's results inform the next query, chaining together evidence.

Reranking with Cross-Encoders

Bi-encoders (embedding models) are fast but independently encode query and document. Cross-encoders process query and document together, enabling richer interaction. They are too slow for searching millions of documents but perfect for reranking a small candidate set.

Figure 7.21 — Cross-encoder reranking re-scores bi-encoder candidates, often significantly changing the ranking.

Self-RAG: When to Retrieve

Not every question needs retrieval. Self-RAG trains the model to decide when retrieval is necessary and to critically evaluate whether retrieved documents are actually relevant.

Figure 7.22 — Self-RAG: the model decides whether to retrieve, then evaluates if retrieved documents are useful.

Self-RAG introduces special tokens during training that signal:

  • [Retrieve] — "I should look this up"
  • [IsRelevant] — "This document is relevant to the query"
  • [IsSupported] — "My answer is supported by the evidence"
  • [IsUseful] — "My response actually helps the user"

RAG vs. Fine-Tuning

A common question: should you use RAG, fine-tune the model, or both? Each approach has distinct strengths.

Dimension RAG Fine-Tuning
Knowledge source External documents (updatable) Baked into model weights
Freshness Always up-to-date (swap docs) Frozen at training time
Traceability Can cite sources No provenance
Cost to update Low (re-index new docs) High (re-train)
Latency Higher (retrieval step) Lower (direct generation)
Best for style/tone Weak Strong
Best for domain facts Strong Moderate (can hallucinate)
Hallucination risk Lower (grounded in docs) Higher (memorization gaps)
Combined approach Fine-tune for style + RAG for facts = best of both worlds
When to Use What

Use RAG when your knowledge changes frequently, you need source attribution, or you have a large corpus. Use fine-tuning when you need a specific style, format, or reasoning pattern. Use both when you need domain-adapted behavior plus dynamic knowledge.

Putting It All Together

A production-grade RAG system combines many of these techniques into a coherent pipeline. Here is a summary of the complete architecture:

  1. Ingestion: Documents are chunked (semantic or fixed-size with overlap), embedded, and stored in a vector database with metadata.
  2. Query processing: The user's query may be rewritten, expanded, or transformed via HyDE before embedding.
  3. Retrieval: ANN search returns top-K candidates from the vector index, potentially across multiple hops.
  4. Reranking: A cross-encoder rescores candidates for precision.
  5. Generation: Top chunks are assembled into a prompt template with careful attention to ordering (best first/last) and context budget.
  6. Self-evaluation: The system may check if its answer is grounded in the retrieved evidence and retry if not.

Each component is independently tunable. The beauty of RAG is its modularity — you can upgrade the embedding model, swap the vector database, or change the reranker without touching the rest of the system. This composability is what makes RAG the dominant paradigm for building knowledge-intensive AI applications.

Chapter 7 Summary

In this chapter, we built RAG from the ground up:

  1. Vector Embeddings turn text into geometric points where semantic similarity maps to spatial proximity. Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors.
  2. Vector Databases use ANN algorithms like HNSW and IVF to search millions of vectors in milliseconds, trading a small accuracy margin for dramatic speed gains.
  3. Chunking Strategies determine how documents are split. Fixed-size, semantic, and overlapping approaches each have trade-offs. The retrieval pipeline (embed, search, rerank) determines what the model sees.
  4. Context-Augmented Generation requires careful prompt construction, context window budgeting, and awareness of attention patterns like the "lost in the middle" problem.
  5. Advanced Patterns like query rewriting, HyDE, multi-hop retrieval, cross-encoder reranking, and Self-RAG push RAG systems toward higher precision and reliability.

RAG is not a single technique — it is a design pattern for grounding language models in external knowledge. Mastering its components gives you the tools to build AI systems that are accurate, up-to-date, and trustworthy.

Chapter 8: MCP from Scratch

The Model Context Protocol — a universal interface between AI models and the world of tools, data, and services.

8.1 — What Is MCP & Why It Exists

The Integration Problem

Imagine you have N different AI models (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama, etc.) and M different tools and data sources (file systems, databases, GitHub, Slack, web search, etc.). Without a standard protocol, every model must build a custom integration for every tool. That means N × M separate connectors — a tangled web of bespoke code.

As either side grows, the complexity explodes. Adding one new model requires M new integrations; adding one new tool requires N. This is unsustainable.

Figure 8.1 — Without a standard protocol, N models × M tools requires N×M custom integrations.

The Solution: MCP as a Universal Protocol

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that provides a single, uniform interface between AI models and external tools. Think of it the way USB standardised hardware connections: before USB, every device had a unique plug; after USB, one plug fits everything. MCP does the same for AI integrations.

With MCP, every model speaks the same protocol, and every tool exposes the same interface. The integration count drops from N × M to N + M — each model implements MCP once, each tool implements MCP once, and they all interoperate.

Figure 8.2 — MCP creates a hub: each side connects once, reducing integrations from N×M to N+M.
Key Insight

MCP was released by Anthropic as an open specification in late 2024. It is model-agnostic — any AI system can be an MCP client, and any service can be an MCP server.

Three Core Capabilities

MCP organises everything an external service can offer into three categories. Together, these cover the full range of interactions an AI model might need.

Tools

Actions the model can invoke — functions with defined inputs and outputs. Examples: run a query, send a message, create a file, execute code.

Resources

Data the model can read — structured or unstructured content accessed via URIs. Examples: file contents, database rows, API responses, log entries.

Prompts

Reusable prompt templates the server can offer. These help the model interact with the service more effectively by providing pre-built interaction patterns.

Not every server needs all three. A file-system server might expose tools (write, delete) and resources (read files), while a code-review server might focus on prompts (templates for reviewing PRs).

Real-World MCP Server Examples

The MCP ecosystem is growing rapidly. Here are some common server types and what they provide:

Filesystem Server

Read, write, search, and manage files and directories on the local machine. Exposes tools like read_file, write_file, list_directory.

Database Server

Query and manipulate databases (PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc.). Tools for running SQL queries; resources for schema information.

GitHub Server

Interact with repositories, issues, pull requests, and code. Tools for creating issues, reading PRs, searching code across repos.

Slack Server

Send and read messages, manage channels, search conversation history. Tools: send_message, search_messages.

Web Search Server

Search the web using APIs like Brave Search or Google. Returns structured results the model can reason about.

Memory Server

Persist knowledge across sessions using a knowledge graph. Store and retrieve entities and relationships for long-term recall.

8.2 — MCP Protocol & Transport

JSON-RPC 2.0 Foundation

MCP is built on JSON-RPC 2.0, a lightweight remote procedure call protocol. Every MCP message is a JSON object with a well-defined structure. There are three message shapes:

Request

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "method": "tools/call",
  "params": {
    "name": "read_file",
    "arguments": {
      "path": "/home/user/notes.txt"
    }
  }
}

Response (Success)

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "result": {
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "Contents of the file..."
      }
    ]
  }
}

Response (Error)

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "error": {
    "code": -32602,
    "message": "File not found: /home/user/notes.txt"
  }
}

Every request carries a unique id so the client can match responses to requests, even if they arrive out of order. The method field tells the server which operation to perform.

Core Message Types

MCP defines a set of standard methods that every client and server understand:

Method Direction Purpose
initialize Client → Server Establish connection; exchange protocol versions and capabilities
initialized Client → Server Notification confirming the client has processed the init response
tools/list Client → Server Discover available tools and their schemas
tools/call Client → Server Invoke a specific tool with arguments
resources/list Client → Server Discover available resources and URI templates
resources/read Client → Server Read data from a resource by URI
prompts/list Client → Server Discover available prompt templates
prompts/get Client → Server Retrieve a specific prompt with arguments filled in
ping Either direction Health check — verify the connection is alive

Transport: Stdio

The simplest MCP transport is stdio (standard input/output). The client spawns the server as a child process and communicates by writing JSON-RPC messages to the server's stdin and reading responses from its stdout. Each message is a single line of JSON terminated by a newline.

Figure 8.3 — Stdio transport: the client spawns the server and uses stdin/stdout pipes for communication.
Why Stdio?

Stdio is ideal for local tools: no network setup, no ports, no authentication. The server process lives and dies with the client session. It is the default transport for most MCP server implementations.

Transport: HTTP + Server-Sent Events (SSE)

For remote servers, MCP supports HTTP with Server-Sent Events. The client sends requests via HTTP POST, and the server streams responses back using SSE. This allows long-running operations and server-initiated notifications.

Figure 8.4 — SSE transport: requests go via HTTP POST, responses stream back as server-sent events.

Message Lifecycle

Regardless of transport, every MCP interaction follows a clear lifecycle. The client initiates a request, the server processes it, and a response flows back.

Figure 8.5 — Typical MCP session: initialize, discover tools, then invoke them as needed.

8.3 — Building an MCP Server

Server Anatomy

An MCP server is a program that listens for JSON-RPC messages and responds according to the MCP specification. At its core, a server has three registries — one for each capability type — plus a message router that dispatches incoming requests.

Figure 8.6 — Internal architecture of an MCP server: transport, router, and three capability registries.

Defining Tools with JSON Schema

Each tool registered on the server has a name, a human-readable description, and an inputSchema that describes its parameters using JSON Schema. This schema is sent to clients during discovery so the model knows exactly what arguments to provide. 

{
  "name": "read_file",
  "description": "Read the contents of a file at the given path.",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "path": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "Absolute path to the file"
      },
      "encoding": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "File encoding (default: utf-8)",
        "enum": ["utf-8", "ascii", "latin-1"]
      }
    },
    "required": ["path"]
  }
}

Here is a more complex tool definition for a database query tool:

{
  "name": "query_database",
  "description": "Execute a read-only SQL query against the database.",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "sql": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "The SQL SELECT query to execute"
      },
      "database": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "Database name to query"
      },
      "limit": {
        "type": "integer",
        "description": "Max rows to return (default: 100)",
        "default": 100
      }
    },
    "required": ["sql", "database"]
  }
}
Why JSON Schema?

JSON Schema is a widely adopted standard for describing JSON data structures. Using it for tool parameters means any model that understands JSON Schema can automatically generate valid tool calls — no custom parsing needed.

Handling Tool Calls

When a tools/call request arrives, the server must route it to the correct handler, validate the arguments, execute the operation, and return the result (or an error). Here is the flow:

Figure 8.7 — How a server processes a tools/call request: lookup, validate, execute, respond.

Resource Endpoints

Resources expose data that the model can read. Each resource has a URI (often using a custom scheme) and a MIME type. The server can offer both static resources (fixed URIs) and dynamic resources (URI templates with placeholders).

Static Resource Example

{
  "uri": "config://app/settings",
  "name": "Application Settings",
  "description": "Current application configuration values",
  "mimeType": "application/json"
}

Dynamic Resource Template

{
  "uriTemplate": "file:///{path}",
  "name": "File Contents",
  "description": "Read any file by its path",
  "mimeType": "text/plain"
}

When the client sends resources/read with a URI like file:///home/user/data.csv, the server matches it against registered templates, extracts the path parameter, reads the file, and returns the content.

Error Handling

MCP uses standard JSON-RPC error codes, plus some protocol-specific ones. Robust error handling is critical because AI models need clear signals to understand what went wrong and whether to retry.

Code Name Meaning
-32700 Parse Error Invalid JSON was received
-32600 Invalid Request The JSON is valid but not a proper JSON-RPC request
-32601 Method Not Found The requested method does not exist on this server
-32602 Invalid Params Arguments do not match the tool's input schema
-32603 Internal Error Server encountered an unexpected failure during execution
-32001 Resource Not Found The requested resource URI does not exist
-32002 Permission Denied The operation is not allowed by the server's security policy

A well-implemented server should always return meaningful error messages alongside the error code, so the model (or human) can understand the problem: 

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 5,
  "error": {
    "code": -32602,
    "message": "Invalid params: 'sql' is required but was not provided",
    "data": {
      "missing_fields": ["sql"],
      "tool": "query_database"
    }
  }
}

8.4 — Building an MCP Client

Client Lifecycle

An MCP client manages the full lifecycle of a connection to one or more servers. The typical flow is: connect to the server, initialize the session, discover capabilities, use tools and resources, and finally disconnect.

Figure 8.8 — The five stages of an MCP client session.

Capability Discovery

After initializing, the client asks the server what it can do. This is the discovery phase. The client sends tools/list, resources/list, and optionally prompts/list to learn what is available.

Discovery Request

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 2,
  "method": "tools/list",
  "params": {}
}

Discovery Response

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 2,
  "result": {
    "tools": [
      {
        "name": "read_file",
        "description": "Read the contents of a file",
        "inputSchema": {
          "type": "object",
          "properties": {
            "path": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Absolute file path"
            }
          },
          "required": ["path"]
        }
      },
      {
        "name": "write_file",
        "description": "Write content to a file",
        "inputSchema": {
          "type": "object",
          "properties": {
            "path": { "type": "string" },
            "content": { "type": "string" }
          },
          "required": ["path", "content"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

The client stores these definitions and presents them to the AI model. When the model decides to use a tool, it constructs arguments that match the schema, and the client sends the tools/call request on its behalf.

Tool Invocation Sequence

Here is the full sequence when an AI model uses an MCP tool during a conversation. The host application acts as the bridge between the model and the MCP server.

Figure 8.9 — Full sequence: user question triggers model to call a tool via MCP, result flows back into the response.

Multi-Server Management

A single MCP client can connect to multiple servers simultaneously. For example, Claude Desktop might connect to a filesystem server, a GitHub server, and a database server at the same time. The client maintains separate connections and merges the available tools into one unified list for the model.

Figure 8.10 — A single client can manage connections to many MCP servers, each with its own transport.

This is how applications like Claude Desktop and Claude Code work: they maintain a configuration file listing all MCP servers to connect to, spawn or connect to each one, and merge all capabilities into a single pool the model can draw from.

Configuration Example

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/home/user"],
      "transport": "stdio"
    },
    "github": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
      "env": { "GITHUB_TOKEN": "ghp_xxxxx" },
      "transport": "stdio"
    },
    "database": {
      "url": "http://localhost:8080/mcp",
      "transport": "sse"
    }
  }
}

The Full MCP Ecosystem

Let us put it all together. The MCP ecosystem has three layers: hosts (applications that embed AI models), clients (protocol-level connectors inside hosts), and servers (services that expose tools, resources, and prompts). This layered architecture keeps each concern separate and composable.

Figure 8.11 — The complete MCP ecosystem: hosts embed clients that connect to servers over a standard protocol.

Chapter Summary

The Model Context Protocol solves the fundamental integration problem in AI: connecting models to tools and data without building bespoke connectors for every combination. By standardising the interface using JSON-RPC 2.0, MCP reduces the integration burden from N×M to N+M.

Key takeaways from this chapter:

The Problem

N models × M tools = an explosion of custom integrations that are expensive to build and maintain.

The Solution

MCP provides a universal protocol layer, reducing complexity to N + M. Each side implements MCP once.

Three Capabilities

Servers expose Tools (actions), Resources (data), and Prompts (templates) through a uniform interface.

JSON-RPC 2.0

All MCP messages use JSON-RPC: requests carry a method and params, responses carry a result or error.

Two Transports

Stdio for local servers (child processes) and HTTP+SSE for remote servers. Same protocol, different pipes.

Ecosystem

Hosts embed clients; clients connect to servers. One client can manage multiple servers simultaneously.

Looking Ahead

In the next chapter, we will build a complete MCP server and client from scratch in Python, implementing the full lifecycle: initialization, tool registration, capability discovery, and tool invocation. We will create a working file-management server that any MCP-compatible host can connect to.

Chapter 9

Build a Small Language Model from Scratch

Knowledge distillation, compression, efficient architectures, and deploying language models on the edge.

Section 9.1

Knowledge Distillation

Large language models like GPT-4 or LLaMA-70B deliver remarkable accuracy, but they demand enormous compute. A 70-billion-parameter model requires multiple high-end GPUs just for inference. Can we transfer that knowledge into a model small enough to run on a phone? That is the promise of knowledge distillation.

The Motivation: Why Small Models?

Consider the contrast between deploying a massive model in a data center versus running a compact model at the edge. The practical differences are staggering in terms of cost, latency, and accessibility.

Figure 9.1 — A 70B model needs 4 GPUs and a data center; a distilled 1B model runs on a phone with only a modest accuracy drop.

The distilled model is 70x smaller, yet retains roughly 97% of the teacher's accuracy. This is not magic — it is the result of carefully transferring the teacher's learned representations into a compact student architecture.

Teacher-Student Framework

Knowledge distillation, introduced by Hinton et al. (2015), uses a two-model setup. A large, already-trained teacher model produces probability distributions over outputs. A smaller student model learns to mimic these distributions, rather than training only on hard ground-truth labels.

Figure 9.2 — The teacher-student distillation pipeline. The student learns to match the teacher's soft output distribution.

Hard Labels vs. Soft Labels

A hard label is a one-hot vector: the correct class gets probability 1, everything else gets 0. But the teacher's soft labels carry far more information. When classifying an image of a cat, the teacher might output [cat: 0.72, tiger: 0.15, dog: 0.10, car: 0.03]. The relationship between "cat" and "tiger" — what Hinton calls "dark knowledge" — is invisible in hard labels but visible in soft ones.

Figure 9.3 — Hard labels discard inter-class relationships. Soft labels from the teacher encode rich similarity structure.
Key Insight

The soft probability distribution tells the student not just what the answer is, but how the teacher reasons about similar classes. A cat is more like a tiger than a car — information that hard labels cannot convey.

Temperature Scaling

Standard softmax produces very peaked distributions: the correct class dominates and the "dark knowledge" in the smaller probabilities is hard to learn from. Temperature scaling softens the distribution by dividing logits by a temperature parameter T before applying softmax.

Temperature-Scaled Softmax
p_i = exp(z_i / T) / sum_j exp(z_j / T)

T = 1 : standard (peaked) distribution
T > 1 : softer, more uniform distribution
T -> inf : uniform distribution
Figure 9.4 — As temperature T increases, the distribution flattens. T = 3-10 is typically used for distillation.

The Distillation Loss Function

The student is trained with a weighted combination of two losses. The first is the KL divergence between the teacher's and student's softened outputs. The second is the standard cross-entropy against the ground-truth hard labels.

Combined Distillation Loss
L = alpha * T^2 * KL(p_teacher(T) || p_student(T)) + (1 - alpha) * CE(y_true, p_student)

where:
  alpha = weight balancing the two losses (typically 0.5-0.9)
  T = temperature (typically 3-10)
  T^2 = scaling factor to correct gradient magnitudes
  KL(...) = Kullback-Leibler divergence (soft target loss)
  CE(...) = cross-entropy (hard target loss)

The T^2 factor is critical: when temperature is raised, the magnitudes of the softmax gradients scale as 1/T^2, so multiplying by T^2 ensures the distillation gradients remain properly scaled relative to the hard-label loss.

DistilBERT and Other Success Stories

Knowledge distillation has produced some of the most successful model compression results in NLP. Below is a summary of notable distilled models and their performance relative to their teachers.

Model Teacher Size Reduction Speed Gain Perf. Retained
DistilBERT BERT-base 40% smaller 1.6x faster 97%
TinyBERT BERT-base 7.5x smaller 9.4x faster 96%
MiniLM BERT-large 4x smaller 5.3x faster 99%
Alpaca (7B) GPT-3.5 25x smaller ~20x cheaper ~90%
Vicuna (13B) GPT-4 ~100x smaller ~50x cheaper ~92%
Practical Note

Modern LLM distillation often uses the teacher to generate training data (response distillation) rather than matching logits directly, since teacher logits may not be accessible for proprietary models like GPT-4.

Section 9.2

Pruning & Quantization

While distillation trains a new small model, pruning and quantization compress an existing model. Pruning removes unnecessary weights; quantization reduces the precision of the remaining ones. Both can be combined for dramatic compression.

Weight Pruning

Many weights in a neural network are close to zero and contribute little to the output. Magnitude pruning sets these small weights to exactly zero, creating a sparse weight matrix. The key insight is that most of the model's capacity is concentrated in a small fraction of its parameters.

Figure 9.5 — Magnitude pruning removes weights with absolute value below a threshold, creating a sparse matrix.

Structured vs. Unstructured Pruning

Unstructured pruning removes individual weights anywhere in the matrix. It achieves high sparsity but requires specialized sparse hardware to realize speed gains. Structured pruning removes entire rows, columns, or attention heads — producing smaller dense matrices that run faster on standard hardware.

Figure 9.6 — Unstructured pruning scatters zeros randomly; structured pruning removes entire columns (or rows/heads).

Quantization: Reducing Precision

Quantization reduces the number of bits used to represent each weight. A standard FP32 weight uses 32 bits; quantizing to INT8 uses only 8 bits, a 4x reduction in memory. More aggressive schemes use 4-bit or even 2-bit representations.

Figure 9.7 — FP32 uses 32 bits per weight; INT8 uses just 8 bits, sacrificing range and precision for a 4x memory reduction.

Post-Training vs. Quantization-Aware Training

There are two main approaches to quantization. Post-training quantization (PTQ) quantizes an already-trained model without any retraining. Quantization-aware training (QAT) simulates quantization during training so the model learns to be robust to lower precision.

Aspect Post-Training (PTQ) QAT
Training required? No (minutes) Yes (full retraining)
Accuracy at INT8 Good (~0.5% drop) Excellent (~0.1% drop)
Accuracy at INT4 Moderate (1-3% drop) Good (~0.5% drop)
Compute cost Very low High (1.2-1.5x training)
Calibration data Small set (~1000 samples) Full training set
Best for Quick deployment Maximum quality

GPTQ, AWQ, and Modern Quantization

Recent quantization methods are tailored specifically for large language models. GPTQ uses approximate second-order information to quantize weights layer by layer with minimal error. AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization) identifies the most important weights by analyzing activation magnitudes and protects them from aggressive quantization.

Method Bits Compression Perplexity Change Speed
Baseline (FP16) 16 1x Baseline 1x
GPTQ 4 4x +0.2-0.5 2-3x
AWQ 4 4x +0.1-0.3 2-3x
GGML/GGUF (Q4_K_M) 4.5 avg 3.5x +0.2-0.4 CPU-friendly
Extreme (2-bit) 2 8x +2-5 4-5x
Rule of Thumb

4-bit quantization (GPTQ/AWQ) is the current sweet spot for LLMs: it cuts memory by 4x with nearly imperceptible quality loss. Going below 4 bits typically requires careful mixed-precision strategies.

Section 9.3

Efficient Attention: GQA & MQA

The attention mechanism is the computational bottleneck of transformer models, especially during inference when the KV-cache grows linearly with sequence length. This section covers architectural innovations that dramatically reduce memory and compute in the attention layer.

Standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA)

In standard multi-head attention, each head has its own separate Query, Key, and Value projection matrices. For a model with h heads, this means h independent sets of K and V matrices, all of which must be cached during autoregressive generation.

Figure 9.8 — Standard MHA: every head maintains independent K and V projections. KV-cache size scales with the number of heads.

Multi-Query Attention (MQA)

Multi-Query Attention, introduced by Shazeer (2019), keeps separate Q projections for each head but shares a single K and V projection across all heads. This dramatically reduces the KV-cache memory from O(h * d) to O(d).

Figure 9.9 — MQA: a single shared K and V is used across all heads, cutting KV-cache by h times.

Grouped Query Attention (GQA)

Grouped Query Attention (Ainslie et al., 2023) is the middle ground between MHA and MQA. Instead of one KV pair for all heads (MQA) or one per head (MHA), GQA uses g groups, where each group of query heads shares a single KV pair. LLaMA 2 (70B) and Mistral use GQA with good results.

Figure 9.10 — GQA with 2 groups: heads 1-4 share one KV pair, heads 5-8 share another. A flexible middle ground.

Memory Savings

The KV-cache is the dominant memory consumer during long-context inference. Here is how the three attention variants compare for a model with 32 heads and 128-dim per head, at sequence length 4096.

Figure 9.11 — KV-cache memory comparison. MQA uses 32x less memory than MHA; GQA offers a balanced trade-off.

KV-Cache Impact at Inference

During autoregressive generation, the KV-cache stores all previously computed key and value vectors. For long sequences, this cache can exceed the model weights in memory usage.

Model Config Attn Type KV-Cache (4K ctx) KV-Cache (32K ctx) KV-Cache (128K ctx)
7B, 32 heads MHA 1.0 GB 8.0 GB 32.0 GB
7B, 32 heads GQA (g=8) 0.25 GB 2.0 GB 8.0 GB
7B, 32 heads MQA 0.03 GB 0.25 GB 1.0 GB
70B, 64 heads MHA 10.0 GB 80.0 GB 320.0 GB
70B, 64 heads GQA (g=8) 1.25 GB 10.0 GB 40.0 GB
Key Insight

GQA with 8 groups has become the de facto standard for modern LLMs (LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, Gemma). It provides 4-8x KV-cache reduction with minimal quality loss compared to full MHA.

Section 9.4

Training a Small Model Effectively

Building a small but capable language model is not just about using fewer parameters. The Chinchilla scaling laws revealed that most models were dramatically undertrained. The key to a good small model is pairing the right amount of parameters with the right amount (and quality) of data, then training efficiently.

Chinchilla Scaling Laws

The DeepMind Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that the optimal ratio of training tokens to parameters is approximately 20:1. A 1B parameter model should be trained on ~20B tokens. Prior models like GPT-3 (175B params, 300B tokens) were significantly undertrained by this standard.

Figure 9.12 — Chinchilla scaling laws: at any compute budget, there is an optimal balance between model size and training data.
Beyond Chinchilla

Recent work (LLaMA, Gemma) shows that for inference-optimized models, it pays to overtrain a smaller model on far more data than Chinchilla suggests. LLaMA-7B was trained on 1T tokens (140:1 ratio) because inference cost matters more than training cost in deployment.

Data Quality Over Quantity

Raw web crawl data is noisy: duplicates, spam, toxic content, and low-quality text. A robust data filtering pipeline is essential. Models trained on 1T filtered tokens routinely outperform models trained on 5T unfiltered tokens.

Figure 9.13 — A multi-stage data filtering pipeline progressively cleans raw web crawl data.

Curriculum Learning
