Curriculum learning presents training data in a structured order: easy examples first, then progressively harder ones. For language models, this might mean starting with simple, well-structured Wikipedia text, then introducing noisy web text, and finally adding complex reasoning-heavy data.

The intuition mirrors how humans learn: master fundamentals before tackling advanced material. Empirically, curriculum learning can improve both convergence speed and final model quality by 1-3% on benchmarks.

Data Mixing

The composition of training data matters enormously. Different data domains contribute different capabilities. The exact mixture is one of the most closely guarded secrets in LLM training.

Figure 9.14 — A typical data mix for training a general-purpose small LLM. Code data improves reasoning even for non-code tasks.

Training Efficiency Techniques

Even with a small model, training on trillions of tokens is expensive. Several techniques reduce the computational and memory cost of training.

Gradient Accumulation

Simulates large batch sizes without requiring large GPU memory. Instead of one forward-backward pass with batch size 1024, perform 8 passes with batch size 128 and accumulate the gradients before updating weights. The effect is mathematically identical.

Mixed Precision Training (BF16/FP16)

Stores weights in FP32 but performs forward and backward passes in BF16 (bfloat16). This halves memory usage for activations and roughly doubles throughput on modern GPUs. BF16 is preferred over FP16 for LLM training because it has the same exponent range as FP32 (8 bits), avoiding overflow issues.

Flash Attention

Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) restructures the attention computation to be IO-aware: it tiles the attention matrix and computes it in SRAM (fast on-chip memory) rather than writing the full N x N attention matrix to slower HBM. This reduces memory from O(N^2) to O(N) and provides a 2-4x wall-clock speedup.

Technique Memory Savings Speed Impact Implementation Effort
Gradient Accumulation Linear with steps Neutral Easy (1 line)
Mixed Precision (BF16) ~50% 1.5-2x faster Easy
Flash Attention v2 O(N^2) to O(N) 2-4x faster Library swap
Gradient Checkpointing ~60-70% ~30% slower Easy
ZeRO Stage 2/3 Linear with GPUs Near-linear scaling Moderate
Key Insight

A well-configured 1B model trained on 50B high-quality tokens with BF16 + Flash Attention on 8 A100 GPUs can be completed in about 3 days for roughly $5,000 in cloud compute. The same quality 5 years ago would have cost 100x more.

Section 9.5

Deployment & Edge Inference

You have a small, well-trained model. Now it needs to run efficiently in production — on servers, phones, Raspberry Pis, and even in web browsers. This section covers the optimization pipeline from trained model to deployed inference engine.

The Optimization Pipeline

Deploying a model involves several transformation steps. Each step reduces latency, memory footprint, or both.

Figure 9.15 — The deployment pipeline: quantize for size, export for portability, compile for speed, serve for production.

Runtime Comparison

Different inference runtimes are optimized for different hardware targets. Choosing the right runtime can make the difference between feasible and infeasible deployment.

Runtime Target Hardware Strengths Best For
vLLM NVIDIA GPU PagedAttention, continuous batching High-throughput server
TensorRT-LLM NVIDIA GPU Graph optimization, kernel fusion Low-latency server
llama.cpp (GGUF) CPU / Metal No GPU required, quantized Desktop, Mac, RPi
ONNX Runtime CPU / GPU / NPU Cross-platform, wide support Cross-platform deploy
CoreML Apple Neural Engine ANE acceleration, on-device iOS / macOS apps
TFLite / MediaPipe Android / ARM Small binary, mobile-optimized Android apps
MLC-LLM Any (via TVM) Universal compilation Any device

Edge Device Comparison

Small language models can now run on a surprisingly wide range of hardware. The key constraints are memory (can the model fit?) and compute (is the latency acceptable for the use case?).

Device RAM Max Model (INT4) Tokens/sec Use Case
iPhone 15 Pro 8 GB ~3B 20-35 On-device assistant
Pixel 8 Pro 12 GB ~3B 15-25 On-device tasks
M2 MacBook Air 16-24 GB ~13B 30-50 Local dev / privacy
Raspberry Pi 5 8 GB ~1B 2-5 IoT / embedded
NVIDIA Jetson Orin 32 GB unified ~13B 40-60 Robotics / edge AI
Browser (WebGPU) ~4 GB usable ~1B 5-15 Privacy-first web apps

WebAssembly & WebGPU: Browser Inference

One of the most exciting frontiers for small models is in-browser inference. Two web technologies make this possible:

  • WebAssembly (Wasm) compiles C/C++ code to a portable binary that runs in any browser. Projects like llama.cpp can be compiled to Wasm for CPU-based inference directly in a web tab.
  • WebGPU provides low-level GPU access from the browser, enabling GPU-accelerated inference. Libraries like web-llm (MLC) run quantized models at 10-15 tokens/sec in Chrome.

The major advantage is zero server cost and complete privacy — the model runs entirely on the user's device. The trade-off is limited model size (typically under 2B parameters) and dependence on the user's hardware.

The Future is On-Device

With Apple, Google, and Qualcomm all adding dedicated AI accelerators (Neural Engine, TPU, Hexagon NPU), on-device LLM inference is becoming mainstream. Models like Gemma 2B and Phi-3 Mini (3.8B) are specifically designed for this deployment scenario.

Latency Benchmarks

The following table shows representative inference latency (time-to-first-token and tokens per second) for a 1B parameter model across different deployment targets, using INT4 quantization.

Deployment Target Runtime Time to First Token Tokens/sec Model Format
A100 GPU (server) vLLM ~12ms 180-220 GPTQ INT4
A100 GPU (server) TensorRT-LLM ~8ms 250-300 TRT engine
M2 Mac (laptop) llama.cpp ~50ms 45-60 GGUF Q4_K_M
iPhone 15 Pro CoreML ~80ms 30-40 CoreML INT4
Pixel 8 Pro MediaPipe ~120ms 20-28 TFLite INT4
Raspberry Pi 5 llama.cpp ~800ms 3-5 GGUF Q4_0
Chrome (WebGPU) web-llm ~200ms 10-18 MLC INT4
Chrome (Wasm CPU) wllama ~500ms 3-6 GGUF Q4_0
Key Insight

A well-optimized 1B model with INT4 quantization produces 30-40 tokens per second on a modern smartphone — fast enough for real-time conversational AI without any network connection. The entire model fits in under 1 GB of memory.

Chapter Summary

Building a small language model from scratch involves a full pipeline of interconnected decisions:

  1. Knowledge Distillation transfers the capabilities of a large teacher model into a compact student through soft-label learning and temperature-scaled loss functions.
  2. Pruning and Quantization compress an existing model by removing unnecessary weights and reducing numerical precision, with INT4 quantization offering the best quality-compression trade-off.
  3. Efficient Attention mechanisms like GQA reduce the KV-cache memory bottleneck by 4-8x, enabling longer contexts on smaller hardware.
  4. Training Strategy matters as much as architecture: Chinchilla-optimal data ratios, high-quality filtered data, curriculum learning, and efficient training techniques (Flash Attention, BF16, gradient accumulation) maximize quality per dollar.
  5. Deployment requires matching the right runtime and format to the target hardware, with modern phones and browsers now capable of running billion-parameter models in real time.

The era of "bigger is always better" is giving way to a more nuanced understanding: the best model is the one that delivers the required quality at the lowest deployment cost. Small, well-trained, efficiently deployed models are the future of practical AI.

Chapter 10

Build an AI Agent from Scratch

From passive chatbots to autonomous reasoning systems
that think, plan, act, and learn

Section 10.1

Agent Architecture & the ReAct Loop

Until now, the language models we have studied are fundamentally reactive: they receive a prompt, generate a completion, and stop. They cannot browse the web, run code, query a database, or take any action in the real world. An AI agent changes this picture entirely.

An agent is an LLM-powered system that can reason about a goal, choose actions to advance toward that goal, observe the results of those actions, and iterate until the goal is achieved. The difference between a chatbot and an agent is the difference between answering a question and solving a problem.

Chatbot vs. Agent

A chatbot operates in a single pass: user message in, assistant response out. An agent operates in a loop — it can call tools, inspect results, reconsider its strategy, and try again. The following diagram makes this contrast clear.

Figure 10.1 — A chatbot processes a single request linearly; an agent loops through think-act-observe cycles.

The key insight is that an agent is not a different model — it is the same LLM wrapped in a program that lets it take actions and feed results back into itself. The intelligence comes from the model; the agency comes from the loop.

The ReAct Pattern

ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) is the foundational pattern for LLM agents, introduced by Yao et al. (2023). The idea is simple yet powerful: interleave chain-of-thought reasoning with tool actions. Instead of the model generating its entire answer in one shot, it alternates between thinking out loud and taking concrete steps.

The ReAct Cycle

Thought — The model reasons about what it knows and what it needs to do next.

Action — The model selects a tool and provides the necessary inputs.

Observation — The tool executes and returns a result.

Repeat — The model incorporates the observation and decides: act again, or respond?

Walkthrough: "What's the weather in Tokyo?"

Let us trace through a complete ReAct interaction. The user asks a simple question, but the LLM has no real-time weather data. It must use a tool.

Thought 1
The user wants the current weather in Tokyo. I don't have real-time data, but I have access to the get_weather tool. I'll call it with location "Tokyo, Japan".
Action 1
Call get_weather(location="Tokyo, Japan")
Observation 1
{"temperature": 18, "unit": "celsius", "condition": "Partly cloudy", "humidity": 62, "wind_speed": "12 km/h"}
Thought 2
I now have the weather data. The temperature is 18°C, partly cloudy with 62% humidity. I have all the information needed to answer the user.
Answer
The weather in Tokyo is currently 18°C and partly cloudy, with 62% humidity and winds at 12 km/h.

Notice how the model reasons before acting (Thought 1), then reasons after observing (Thought 2). This interleaving is what separates ReAct from simple function calling — the model has a chance to reflect, catch mistakes, and adjust its plan at every step.

Agent Components

A fully capable agent is built from several interconnected components. At the center is the LLM — the "brain" that does the reasoning. Around it are the subsystems that give it capabilities.

Figure 10.2 — The five core components of an AI agent architecture.

Let us briefly define each component:

  • LLM (Brain) — The reasoning engine. It interprets user goals, generates plans, selects tools, and formulates responses.
  • Tools — External functions the agent can call: web search, calculators, APIs, code execution, database queries.
  • Memory — Storage for conversation history (short-term), learned facts (long-term), and current task state (working memory).
  • Planner — The subsystem that breaks complex goals into smaller, manageable steps before execution begins.
  • Executor — The runtime that actually invokes tools, manages retries, and returns structured results to the LLM.

The Agent Loop

All of these components work together in a single unified loop. This loop is the heartbeat of every agent system, regardless of the framework used. Here it is as a flowchart.

Figure 10.3 — The agent loop: think, decide, act, observe, update, repeat.

This loop can execute many iterations for a single user request. A complex research question might require 10 or more tool calls before the agent has enough information to produce a comprehensive answer. The key design decision is the stopping condition: the model must decide when it has gathered enough information and should stop looping.

Why Not Just Chain Prompts?

You might wonder why we need a formal loop rather than just chaining a few prompts together. The answer is adaptability. A hard-coded chain cannot handle unexpected results, errors, or situations the developer didn't anticipate. The agent loop lets the LLM dynamically decide what to do next based on what actually happened — just like a human problem-solver.

Section 10.2

Tool Use & Function Calling

Tools are the agent's hands. Without tools, an LLM can only generate text. With tools, it can search the internet, execute code, query databases, send emails, call APIs, and interact with virtually any software system. Tool use is what transforms a language model from a thinker into a doer.

What Are Tools?

In the context of AI agents, a tool is simply a function with a well-defined interface. The LLM does not execute the function directly — it generates a structured request (typically JSON) specifying which function to call and what arguments to pass. The agent runtime then executes the function and returns the result to the LLM.

Common tools include:

  • Web Search — Query a search engine and retrieve results
  • Calculator — Perform precise mathematical computations
  • Code Interpreter — Execute Python code in a sandboxed environment
  • File Operations — Read, write, and modify files
  • API Calls — Interact with external services (weather, stocks, databases)
  • Browser — Navigate web pages and extract content

Defining Tools with JSON Schema

For the LLM to use a tool, it needs to know three things: the tool's name, what the tool does (description), and what inputs it accepts (parameters). This information is provided as a JSON Schema definition that is included in the system prompt. 

{
  "name": "get_weather",
  "description": "Get the current weather for a given location. Returns temperature, conditions, humidity, and wind speed.",
  "parameters": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "location": {
        "type": "string",
        "description": "City and country, e.g. 'Tokyo, Japan'"
      },
      "units": {
        "type": "string",
        "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"],
        "description": "Temperature unit (default: celsius)"
      }
    },
    "required": ["location"]
  }
}

The model reads this schema and, when it decides to use the tool, generates a matching JSON object: 

{
  "tool": "get_weather",
  "arguments": {
    "location": "Tokyo, Japan",
    "units": "celsius"
  }
}

Notice the clean separation of concerns: the LLM decides what to do; the runtime handles how to do it. The model never sees the implementation of get_weather — it only knows the interface.

Function Calling Flow

The complete function calling pipeline has four stages. The LLM produces a structured tool call, the executor runs the function, the result is formatted, and the LLM receives it as a new observation.

Figure 10.4 — The four stages of function calling: generate, parse, execute, return.

Parallel Tool Calls

Modern agent frameworks support parallel tool calling, where the LLM can request multiple tools in a single turn. For example, if the user asks "Compare the weather in Tokyo and London," the agent can call get_weather for both cities simultaneously rather than sequentially.

Figure 10.5 — Parallel tool calling: the LLM requests three tools simultaneously, and results are aggregated before being returned.

Parallel execution reduces latency significantly. If each tool call takes 2 seconds, calling three tools sequentially takes 6 seconds, while calling them in parallel takes only 2 seconds.

Tool Design Best Practices

Poorly designed tools are the number-one cause of agent failures. The following guidelines help you create tools that LLMs can use reliably.

Guideline Description
Clear naming Use descriptive, verb-first names like search_web, get_weather, create_file. Avoid ambiguous names.
Detailed descriptions Explain what the tool does, when to use it, and when not to use it. The model relies heavily on the description.
Typed parameters Use JSON Schema types (string, number, boolean) and mark required fields. Include enum for constrained values.
Minimal inputs Only require what's strictly necessary. Use sensible defaults for optional parameters.
Structured output Return JSON with consistent field names. Include an error field for failures rather than throwing exceptions.
Idempotent when possible Calling the same tool with the same arguments should produce the same result. This lets the agent safely retry on failures.
Scope-limited Each tool should do one thing well. A search_and_summarize mega-tool is harder for the model to reason about than separate search and summarize tools.
Common Pitfall: Too Many Tools

Giving an agent 50+ tools might seem powerful, but it degrades performance. The LLM must reason over all available tools every turn, and the probability of selecting the wrong tool increases. In practice, 5-15 well-designed tools outperform 50 mediocre ones. Use tool categories and dynamic tool loading for large tool sets.

Section 10.3

Memory & State Management

An agent without memory is like a person with amnesia: it can reason brilliantly in the moment, but it forgets everything between turns. Memory gives agents the ability to maintain context across interactions, learn from past experiences, and build up knowledge over time.

Three Types of Memory

Agent memory systems are typically organized into three categories, inspired by cognitive science research on human memory.

1. Short-Term Memory (Conversation Buffer)

This is the simplest form: the raw conversation history. Every user message, assistant response, tool call, and observation is stored in a list of messages. This buffer is included in the LLM's context window on every turn. Short-term memory is high fidelity but limited capacity — once the context window fills up, the oldest messages must be dropped or summarized.

2. Long-Term Memory (Vector Store)

For information that should persist across sessions, agents use a vector database. Key facts, user preferences, and past interactions are encoded as embedding vectors and stored in a database like Pinecone, Weaviate, or ChromaDB. When the agent needs to recall something, it performs a semantic search: "What did the user say about their favorite programming language?" returns the most relevant stored memory, even if the exact wording was different.

3. Working Memory (Scratchpad)

Working memory holds the agent's current state: the plan it is executing, intermediate results, and any variables it needs to track. Think of it as a mental scratchpad. Unlike short-term memory (which is a record of what happened), working memory is a structured representation of where the agent is right now in its task.

Figure 10.6 — The three types of agent memory and how they interact with the agent core.

Memory Retrieval: Semantic Search

When an agent has hundreds or thousands of stored memories, it cannot include them all in the context window. Instead, it uses semantic search to find the most relevant memories for the current task.

The process works as follows:

  1. The current query or task description is embedded into a vector using the same embedding model that was used to store the memories.
  2. A nearest-neighbor search finds the top-k most similar memory vectors in the database.
  3. The corresponding text is retrieved and injected into the LLM's context as "recalled memories."

This is essentially RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) applied to the agent's own past experiences rather than an external document corpus.

Memory Consolidation

Over time, short-term memory grows too large for the context window. Memory consolidation solves this by periodically summarizing older conversations and storing the summaries in long-term memory. 

# Pseudo-code for memory consolidation
if len(conversation_buffer) > MAX_BUFFER_SIZE:
    # Summarize the oldest messages
    oldest_messages = conversation_buffer[:CONSOLIDATION_BATCH]
    summary = llm.summarize(oldest_messages)

    # Store summary in long-term memory
    long_term_memory.store(
        text=summary,
        embedding=embed(summary),
        metadata={"timestamp": now(), "type": "consolidation"}
    )

    # Remove oldest messages from buffer
    conversation_buffer = conversation_buffer[CONSOLIDATION_BATCH:]

This mimics how human memory works: detailed short-term memories gradually consolidate into broader, more abstract long-term memories. The agent remembers the gist of earlier interactions without retaining every word.

Memory vs. Context Window: The Tradeoffs

Dimension Context Window External Memory
Capacity Fixed (e.g., 128K tokens) Virtually unlimited
Speed Direct attention — fast Requires retrieval step
Fidelity Perfect (verbatim text) Lossy (search may miss)
Persistence Within session only Across sessions
Cost Increases with size (tokens billed) Storage + embedding cost
Best for Recent context, active task Historical knowledge, user prefs
Design Principle: Hybrid Memory

The best agent systems use all three memory types together. Short-term memory holds the current conversation. Working memory tracks the active plan. Long-term memory stores learned facts and preferences. The agent dynamically retrieves from long-term memory when it recognizes it needs historical information.

Section 10.4

Planning & Task Decomposition

Simple tasks like "What's the weather?" need no planning. But complex tasks like "Research the top 5 competitors in the EV market and write a comparison report" require breaking the goal into subtasks, ordering them logically, and executing them step by step. Planning is what separates toy demos from production agents.

Why Planning Matters

Without planning, an agent using the ReAct loop tends to take a greedy, short-sighted approach: it does whatever seems most useful right now, without considering the overall strategy. This often leads to:

  • Repeating work it already did
  • Missing important subtasks
  • Getting stuck in loops
  • Producing incomplete or disorganized results

Planning introduces foresight. Before the agent starts acting, it creates a structured plan that guides its actions toward the goal.

Task Decomposition

The most common planning strategy is hierarchical task decomposition: recursively breaking a large task into smaller, more manageable subtasks until each subtask is simple enough to execute with a single tool call or LLM generation.

Figure 10.7 — Hierarchical task decomposition: a complex goal is broken into subtasks, then into atomic actions.

Plan-and-Execute Pattern

The Plan-and-Execute pattern separates planning from execution into two distinct phases. First, a "planner" LLM creates a complete step-by-step plan. Then, an "executor" LLM carries out each step one by one, feeding results back for potential replanning.

Figure 10.8 — Plan-and-Execute: the planner creates a full plan, the executor runs it, and results can trigger replanning.

ReWOO: Plan First, Execute Later

ReWOO (Reasoning Without Observation) takes the plan-and-execute idea further. Instead of interleaving planning and execution, it generates the entire plan upfront — including which tools to call and how to use their results — before executing anything. The key innovation is using placeholder variables for tool outputs. 

# ReWOO-style plan with placeholders
Plan:
  Step 1: search_web("EV market share 2025") -> $result1
  Step 2: search_web("Tesla vs BYD sales 2025") -> $result2
  Step 3: extract_data($result1, "market share percentages") -> $data1
  Step 4: compare($data1, $result2) -> $comparison
  Step 5: write_report($comparison, format="markdown") -> $report

# Execute all steps, substituting variables as they resolve
# Total LLM calls: 1 (planning) + 1 (final synthesis)
# vs. ReAct: potentially 10+ LLM calls

The advantage of ReWOO is efficiency: it requires far fewer LLM calls because the model does not need to re-reason after each tool call. The disadvantage is brittleness: if an early step fails or returns unexpected results, the entire plan may be invalid.

Adaptive Replanning

Real-world tasks rarely go exactly as planned. A web search might return no results, an API might time out, or an intermediate result might reveal that the original plan was based on a wrong assumption. Robust agents need to detect failures and replan.

Figure 10.9 — Adaptive replanning: when a step fails, the agent decides whether to retry or generate an entirely new plan.
Planning Tradeoff: Rigidity vs. Flexibility

ReWOO is efficient but rigid — it cannot adapt to surprises. ReAct is flexible but expensive — it re-reasons on every step. Plan-and-Execute with adaptive replanning strikes the best balance: plan ahead for efficiency, but replan when reality diverges from expectations. Most production systems use this hybrid approach.

Section 10.5

Multi-Agent Systems

A single agent can be remarkably capable, but some tasks benefit from specialization. Just as a software team has designers, developers, and testers, a multi-agent system assigns different roles to different agents, each optimized for a specific type of work.

Why Multiple Agents?

There are three main reasons to use multiple agents instead of one:

  1. Specialization — Each agent has its own system prompt, tools, and expertise. A "researcher" agent with web search tools works differently from a "coder" agent with a code interpreter.
  2. Parallel execution — Multiple agents can work on independent subtasks simultaneously, reducing total execution time.
  3. Separation of concerns — Complex system prompts that try to handle everything are fragile. Splitting responsibilities across agents makes each one more reliable.

Communication Patterns

Multi-agent systems differ primarily in how agents communicate. There are three fundamental patterns.

Figure 10.10 — Three fundamental multi-agent communication patterns.

Role Specialization

In practice, the most effective multi-agent systems assign distinct roles to each agent. Each role has its own system prompt, personality, tools, and expertise. Here are three common agent roles.

R

Researcher

Gathers information from web search, documents, and databases. Focuses on finding accurate, relevant sources.

C

Coder

Writes, debugs, and tests code. Has access to a code interpreter and can install packages and run scripts.

V

Reviewer

Evaluates work from other agents. Checks for accuracy, completeness, style, and potential issues. Provides feedback.

Each agent operates independently but communicates through a shared protocol. The researcher finds information, the coder implements solutions, and the reviewer verifies quality — just like a human team.

Orchestration: The Supervisor Pattern

The most popular multi-agent architecture uses a supervisor agent that coordinates worker agents. The supervisor receives the user's request, decomposes it into subtasks, delegates each subtask to the appropriate worker, collects results, and synthesizes the final response.

Figure 10.11 — The supervisor pattern: one agent coordinates and delegates to specialized workers.

Agent Handoff Protocols

When one agent finishes its subtask and passes work to another, the handoff must include all the context the next agent needs. A well-designed handoff protocol includes:

Handoff Field Description
task_description What the receiving agent should do, in plain language
context Relevant information gathered so far (summaries, data, prior results)
constraints Any limitations: time budgets, formatting requirements, scope boundaries
expected_output What format and content the result should have
fallback What to do if the task cannot be completed (return to supervisor, try alternative) 
# Example handoff message
{
  "from": "supervisor",
  "to": "researcher",
  "task_description": "Find the top 5 EV companies by global sales in 2025",
  "context": "User wants a comparison report. Focus on BEV (battery electric) only.",
  "constraints": {
    "max_sources": 10,
    "prefer_recent": true,
    "time_budget_seconds": 30
  },
  "expected_output": {
    "format": "json",
    "fields": ["company_name", "sales_volume", "market_share", "source_url"]
  },
  "fallback": "Return partial results with a note about what's missing"
}
Multi-Agent Complexity Warning

Multi-agent systems are powerful but introduce significant complexity: coordination overhead, debugging difficulty, and compounding errors. Start with a single agent and only add more when you have a clear reason. A well-designed single agent often outperforms a poorly-designed multi-agent system.

Section 10.6

Building the Complete Agent

We have now covered all the pieces: the ReAct loop, tool use, memory, planning, and multi-agent coordination. In this final section, we wire everything together into a complete, production-ready agent architecture.

Full Architecture

The following diagram shows how every component connects in a complete agent system. The user's message enters at the top, flows through the planning, reasoning, and action layers, and the final response exits at the bottom.

Figure 10.12 — Complete agent architecture: input processing, planning, reasoning, tool execution, memory, and response generation.

The Main Loop: Pseudocode

Here is the complete agent loop expressed as clean pseudocode. This is the algorithm at the heart of every agent framework. 

def agent_loop(user_message, tools, memory):
    # 1. Initialize
    messages = memory.get_short_term()
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})

    # 2. Retrieve relevant long-term memories
    relevant_memories = memory.search(user_message, top_k=5)
    system_context = format_memories(relevant_memories)

    # 3. Create plan (optional, for complex tasks)
    plan = planner.create_plan(user_message, system_context)

    # 4. Agent loop
    max_iterations = 10
    for i in range(max_iterations):

        # 4a. Call LLM with current context
        response = llm.generate(
            system_prompt=AGENT_SYSTEM_PROMPT + system_context,
            messages=messages,
            tools=tools
        )

        # 4b. Check if LLM wants to use a tool
        if response.has_tool_calls:
            for tool_call in response.tool_calls:
                # Execute the tool
                result = executor.run(tool_call)

                # Add observation to messages
                messages.append({
                    "role": "tool",
                    "name": tool_call.name,
                    "content": str(result)
                })

            # Update working memory with results
            memory.update_working(plan, step=i, results=result)

        else:
            # 4c. LLM produced a final response
            final_answer = response.content
            break

    # 5. Save to memory
    memory.save_short_term(messages)
    memory.save_long_term(
        summary=llm.summarize(messages[-10:]),
        embedding=embed(user_message + final_answer)
    )

    return final_answer

Study this pseudocode carefully. Every real agent framework (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, custom solutions) implements some variation of this loop. The details differ, but the structure is always: receive, think, decide, act or respond, observe, update, repeat.

Evaluation Benchmarks

How do we know if our agent works well? Several benchmarks exist for evaluating agent performance across different capability dimensions.

Benchmark What It Tests Key Metrics
GAIA General AI assistants on real-world questions requiring tools Accuracy, tool usage correctness
WebArena Autonomous web browsing and task completion Task success rate, steps to completion
SWE-bench Resolving real GitHub issues by writing code Percentage of issues resolved correctly
ToolBench Using 16,000+ real-world APIs Pass rate, API selection accuracy
AgentBench 8 environments including OS, database, web Overall score across environments
HumanEval (Agents) Code generation with iterative debugging pass@k with tool use

Common Failure Modes

Understanding how agents fail is as important as understanding how they succeed. Here are the most common failure modes and their mitigations.

Hallucinated Tool Calls

The agent invents a tool that does not exist or calls a real tool with fabricated parameters. This often happens when the model's training data includes different tool interfaces.

Mitigation: Strict schema validation. Only accept tool calls that exactly match defined schemas. Return clear error messages for invalid calls.

Infinite Loops

The agent gets stuck repeating the same action, often because it does not recognize that the tool result is unhelpful or because it keeps retrying a failed approach.

Mitigation: Set a maximum iteration limit (e.g., 10 steps). Track action history and detect repetition. Force the agent to respond after hitting the limit.

Wrong Tool Selection

The agent picks the wrong tool for the job — using web search when it should use a calculator, or calling an API with the wrong endpoint.

Mitigation: Write detailed tool descriptions with "when to use" and "when NOT to use" guidance. Reduce tool count. Use few-shot examples in the system prompt.

Context Window Overflow

After many tool calls, the accumulated messages exceed the context window limit. The agent loses access to earlier context and may repeat work or contradict itself.

Mitigation: Implement memory consolidation. Summarize older messages. Use sliding window with important messages pinned. Track token usage proactively.

Premature Termination

The agent responds to the user before gathering enough information, producing an incomplete or shallow answer.

Mitigation: Include explicit instructions in the system prompt to verify completeness before responding. Use a reviewer agent or self-critique step.

Production Considerations

Building a demo agent is easy; building a production agent is hard. Here are the critical considerations for deploying agents in real-world applications.

1
Rate Limits & Costs

Each loop iteration costs an API call. A 10-step agent interaction can cost 10x more than a single chatbot response. Implement cost tracking, budget limits per request, and caching for repeated tool calls.

2
Latency

Sequential tool calls add up. A 5-step agent with 2-second tool calls takes 10+ seconds. Use parallel tool calling, streaming responses, and progress indicators to manage user expectations.

3
Safety Guardrails

Agents can take real actions: sending emails, writing files, making purchases. Implement human-in-the-loop confirmation for high-stakes actions, sandbox execution environments, and action whitelists.

4
Observability & Logging

Log every thought, action, observation, and decision. When an agent fails, you need a complete trace to debug the issue. Tools like LangSmith, Arize, or custom logging are essential.

5
Error Recovery

Design graceful degradation: if a tool fails, the agent should try alternatives or inform the user, not crash silently. Return partial results when full completion isn't possible.

6
Testing

Unit test individual tools. Integration test common agent flows. Use evaluation datasets with known-good answers. Monitor production performance with automated quality checks.

Summary: The Complete Agent

Let us close with a high-level summary of everything we have covered in this chapter. An AI agent is built from five core capabilities, wired together by the ReAct loop.

Figure 10.13 — Summary: the five pillars of a complete AI agent, unified by the ReAct loop.
Chapter 10 Key Takeaways

1. An agent is an LLM wrapped in a loop that can reason, act, observe, and update.

2. The ReAct pattern interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with tool actions.

3. Tools are functions with JSON Schema interfaces — the LLM generates structured calls, the runtime executes them.

4. Memory comes in three forms: short-term (buffer), long-term (vector store), and working (scratchpad).

5. Planning decomposes complex goals into executable steps; adaptive replanning handles failures.

6. Multi-agent systems use specialized roles with structured handoff protocols.

7. Production agents need guardrails, logging, cost controls, and graceful error recovery.

The field of AI agents is evolving rapidly. New architectures, tool protocols (like MCP — Model Context Protocol), and evaluation frameworks emerge regularly. But the fundamentals covered in this chapter — the loop, the tools, the memory, the planning — will remain the building blocks of every agent system, no matter how sophisticated the field becomes.

In the next chapter, we will put these ideas into practice by building a working agent from scratch, complete with web search, code execution, and memory persistence.

Chapter 11

Build a Coding Agent from Scratch

How Claude Code Works — From Terminal Input to Autonomous Code Changes

In the previous chapters, you learned about tool use, function calling, and the Model Context Protocol. Now it is time to see how all of those ideas come together in a real, production-grade coding agent that millions of developers use every day: Claude Code.

Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding assistant. It lives in your terminal, reads your entire codebase, runs commands, edits files, and reasons about complex software tasks — all driven by Claude. In this chapter we will take it apart piece by piece so you understand exactly how to build something like it yourself.

By the end you will know: (1) the architecture of an agentic harness, (2) how the agentic loop works, (3) the five categories of tools a coding agent needs, (4) how to manage a finite context window across a long coding session, (5) the safety and permission systems that keep the user in control, and (6) how to extend an agent with skills, hooks, subagents, and MCP servers.

Section 11.1

What Is Claude Code?

Claude Code is an agentic coding assistant that runs directly in your terminal. You launch it with a single command — claude — and then describe what you want in plain English. Claude Code reads your project, reasons about it, makes changes, runs tests, and reports the results, all without leaving the command line.

Unlike inline code assistants that work inside a single file in your editor, Claude Code has visibility into your entire project — every file, every directory, every configuration. It can trace a bug from the frontend through the API layer and into the database migration, because it sees the whole picture at once.

Inline Assistant vs. Agentic Assistant

Think of the difference like this: an inline code assistant is a helpful co-pilot sitting in the passenger seat, seeing only the road directly ahead. An agentic assistant like Claude Code is in the driver's seat with a full GPS map of the entire city. It can navigate anywhere, take detours when necessary, and verify it has arrived at the right destination.

Feature Inline Code Assistant Claude Code (Agentic)
Scope of vision Single file or selection Entire project / codebase
Runs commands No Yes — shell, tests, build, git
Multi-step reasoning Limited Full agentic loop
File creation / editing Suggestions only Direct edits with checkpoints
Runs where Inside an editor plugin Terminal (any environment)
Self-verification None Runs tests, checks types

The Tech Stack

Claude Code is built with a modern stack chosen for speed and developer experience:

  • TypeScript — the primary language, providing type safety and maintainability across a large codebase
  • React + Ink — React components rendered to the terminal (not the browser!). Ink is a library that brings the React component model to CLI applications, making the terminal UI declarative and composable.
  • Yoga — a layout engine (the same one React Native uses) that handles terminal UI positioning with a flexbox-like model
  • Bun — a fast JavaScript runtime that replaces Node.js, offering faster startup and execution

This means the terminal interface you see when you use Claude Code is actually a React application — just one that renders characters and colors instead of HTML elements. Every status indicator, progress bar, and diff display is a React component.

A Self-Building System

Here is a remarkable fact: 90% of the code in Claude Code was written by Claude Code itself. The team at Anthropic uses Claude Code to develop Claude Code. This is not a marketing claim — it is a fundamental part of the development process. The agent writes its own features, fixes its own bugs, and refactors its own architecture. When you use a coding agent to build itself, every improvement to the agent's capabilities directly accelerates the next improvement.

The Core Idea: An Agentic Harness

Claude (the model) is powerful, but it cannot, on its own, read files from your disk or run a shell command. A language model receives text as input and produces text as output — nothing more. It needs an agentic harness — the surrounding software that gives the model hands, eyes, and a workspace. Claude Code is that harness. It provides four essential capabilities:

  • Tools — functions the model can call to interact with the real world (read files, edit code, run bash commands, search the web)
  • Context management — deciding what information the model sees and when, managing the finite context window
  • Execution environment — a controlled sandbox where commands run and results are captured safely
  • Safety layer — permissions, checkpoints, and user control to keep the agent within safe bounds
Figure 11.1 — The four layers of Claude Code's architecture.

When you type a request into the terminal, it flows through these layers: the terminal captures your input, the agentic harness adds context and routes the request, Claude reasons about the task and decides which tools to use, and tools execute actions in your real file system. The results flow back through the same path, with Claude deciding what to do next until the task is complete.

Notice the bidirectional arrow between the harness and Claude. This is the agentic loop in action — the harness sends information to Claude, Claude responds with tool calls, the harness executes those tools and sends the results back, and the cycle continues until the task is done. We will explore this loop in detail in the next section.

Key Concept

An agentic harness is the software wrapper around a language model that gives it the ability to take actions in the real world. Without the harness, the model can only produce text. With the harness, it becomes an agent that can read, write, execute, and verify. Building an agent is fundamentally about building a good harness.

How Claude Code Sees Your Project

When you launch Claude Code in a project directory, it does not immediately read every file. Instead, it starts with a high-level understanding and drills deeper as needed. Here is the typical sequence:

  1. Read CLAUDE.md (if it exists) for project-level instructions
  2. Look at the directory structure using Glob to understand the project layout
  3. Read specific files based on your request and the project structure
  4. Search for patterns using Grep when it needs to find something specific

This lazy, demand-driven approach is critical. A large project might have thousands of files. Reading them all would fill the context window immediately. Instead, Claude reads only what it needs, when it needs it — just like a human developer exploring an unfamiliar codebase.

Section 11.2

The Agentic Loop

At the heart of every coding agent is a loop. This is the engine that drives autonomous behavior — the mechanism that lets Claude go from a single instruction like "fix the failing tests" to a series of coordinated actions that actually solve the problem.

Claude Code's agentic loop has three phases that blend together as the model works through a task:

  1. Gather Context — Read files, search code, run commands to understand the current state
  2. Take Action — Edit files, create new code, run build commands
  3. Verify Results — Run tests, check for type errors, review the output

These phases are not rigid, sequential steps. Claude fluidly moves between them, using tools throughout. A question about your codebase might only need context gathering. A bug fix will cycle through all three phases multiple times. A refactoring task might gather, act, verify, then gather more context when it discovers something unexpected — and loop again.

Figure 11.2 — The three-phase agentic loop with user control.

How the Loop Actually Executes

Under the hood, the agentic loop is implemented as a while loop that keeps running until the model decides it is finished. Each iteration looks roughly like this: 

while (not done) {
    // 1. Send the current context to Claude
    response = callClaude(conversation_history)

    // 2. Check if Claude wants to use a tool
    if (response.has_tool_call) {
        // 3. Execute the tool and get the result
        result = executeTool(response.tool_call)

        // 4. Add the result to conversation history
        conversation_history.append(result)

        // Continue the loop — Claude will decide next step
    } else {
        // Claude is done — show the final response
        displayToUser(response.text)
        done = true
    }
}

This is the same tool-use loop you learned about in earlier chapters, but applied to real coding tasks. Each call to Claude might result in reading a file, editing code, or running a command. The loop continues until Claude produces a text response without any tool calls, signaling that the task is complete.

Example Walkthrough: "Fix the Failing Tests"

Let us trace through a concrete example. You type: Fix the failing tests. Here is how Claude Code's agentic loop processes this request, step by step:

1
Gather: Claude runs npm test via the Bash tool to see which tests are failing. It reads the test output to identify the specific error messages, test names, and file locations.
2
Gather: Claude reads the failing test files and the source files they import, building a mental model of what the code is supposed to do versus what it actually does.
3
Act: Claude edits the source code to fix the first bug — perhaps a function was returning the wrong type after a recent refactor. It uses the Edit tool to make a targeted change.
4
Verify: Claude runs npm test again to check if the fix worked. Two tests now pass, but one still fails with a different error.
5
Gather + Act: Claude reads the still-failing test more carefully, realizes it references an import path that changed in the refactor, and edits the import statement.
6
Verify: Claude runs the tests one final time. All tests pass. Claude reports the fix to the user with a summary of what changed and why.

Notice how the phases blend together. Step 5 combines gathering and acting in a single move — Claude reads a file and edits it in the same logical step. Step 1 uses the Bash tool (an "action") but in service of gathering context. This fluidity is what makes an agentic loop different from a rigid pipeline.

How the Loop Adapts to Different Tasks

Different tasks use the loop differently. The agent is not following a fixed script — it dynamically adjusts its behavior based on what the task requires:

Task Type Primary Phases Typical Loop Iterations
"How does the auth system work?" Gather only 1 (read files, explain)
"Fix this bug" Gather → Act → Verify 2-5 (iterate until tests pass)
"Refactor this module" All three, heavily 5-15 (many edits, many checks)
"Add a new feature with tests" All three, heavily 10-30+ (complex multi-file work)
"Review this PR for issues" Gather + light Verify 1-3 (read the diff, run tests)

User Interruption and Steering

A critical feature of Claude Code's loop is that the user can interrupt at any point. If Claude is heading in the wrong direction — say, refactoring a file that should not be touched, or using the wrong testing framework — you can press Escape, type new instructions, and steer the agent back on course.

This is not a "cancel and restart." Claude keeps its entire context — everything it has read, every change it has made — and adjusts its approach based on your feedback. The conversation continues from where you interrupted, just with a new direction.

Why Interruption Matters

Without user control, an agent might spend minutes going down the wrong path — editing files that should not be touched, or trying an approach that will not work in your specific context. Interruption turns a monologue into a conversation. The best agentic workflows are collaborative: human judgment steering AI capability, AI speed amplifying human expertise.

Key Concept

The agentic loop is not just "call the model in a loop." It is the combination of (1) a reasoning model that decides what to do next, (2) tools that execute those decisions in the real world, and (3) human oversight that can redirect the loop at any time. Remove any of these three elements and the system breaks down.

Section 11.3

Tools — The Hands of the Agent

An agent without tools is just a chatbot. Tools are what let Claude Code do things in the real world — read your files, edit your code, run your tests, search the web. They are the bridge between reasoning and action.

Claude Code provides five categories of tools, each serving a distinct purpose in the coding workflow. Together, they cover everything a developer does day-to-day.

Figure 11.3 — The five categories of tools available to Claude Code.

1. File Operations

File operations are the most frequently used tools. They let Claude interact with your actual file system — the same files you see in your editor and your git repository:

  • Read — Read the contents of a file. Can read specific line ranges to avoid loading entire large files into context.
  • Edit — Make targeted edits to specific parts of a file. Rather than rewriting the whole file, Claude sends only the lines that need to change — a diff. This is safer, faster, and makes it easy to review exactly what changed.
  • Write — Create new files or completely overwrite existing ones. Used for new files that do not exist yet.
  • Glob — Find files matching a pattern (e.g., **/*.test.ts finds all TypeScript test files). Essential for understanding project structure.

The Edit tool deserves special attention. When a human developer fixes a bug, they do not rewrite the entire file — they change a few specific lines. Claude Code works the same way. The Edit tool sends a precise diff, which means:

  • Only the changed lines are modified — the rest of the file is untouched
  • The diff is visible to the user for review before or after application
  • Checkpoints can track exactly what changed
  • Less data is sent, so it is faster and uses less context

2. Search

Search tools let Claude explore your codebase efficiently without reading every file. In a project with thousands of files, these tools are how Claude finds what it needs:

  • Grep — Search file contents using regular expressions. Find every place a function is called, every import of a module, every TODO comment. This is like running grep -r across your entire project.
  • Glob — Find files by name pattern. Locate all TypeScript files, all test files, all configuration files matching *.config.*. This is how Claude discovers what files exist without reading them all.

Search is the foundation of context gathering. Before Claude can fix a bug, it needs to find where the relevant code lives. Before it can refactor a function, it needs to find every place that function is called. Search tools make these operations fast and efficient.

3. Execution

The Bash tool is Claude Code's most powerful and versatile capability. It can run any shell command your system supports:

  • Run test suites: npm test, pytest, cargo test, go test ./...
  • Build projects: npm run build, make, cargo build
  • Git operations: git status, git diff, git commit, git log
  • Install dependencies: npm install, pip install
  • System inspection: check running processes, inspect environment variables, verify ports
Safety Note

The Bash tool is gated by the permissions system. In default mode, Claude will ask for your approval before running commands that could modify your system. Destructive operations like rm -rf or git push --force always require explicit permission. More on this in Section 11.5.

4. Web

When Claude needs information beyond your codebase — perhaps documentation for a library, the syntax of an unfamiliar API, or the solution to an obscure error message — it can reach out to the web:

  • WebSearch — Search the web for documentation, Stack Overflow answers, API references, and best practices
  • WebFetch — Fetch the contents of a specific URL to read documentation pages, README files, or API specs

These tools are especially valuable when Claude encounters a library or framework it needs more detail about. Rather than guessing, it can look up the official documentation in real time.

5. Code Intelligence

Code intelligence tools provide IDE-like capabilities for understanding code structure at a semantic level, beyond simple text search:

  • Diagnostics — Get type errors, linting issues, and compiler warnings for a file. This lets Claude verify its edits are type-safe without running the full build.
  • Go to Definition — Jump to where a function, type, or variable is defined. This is how Claude traces through call chains and understands abstractions.
  • Find References — Find every place a symbol is used across the entire project. Essential for safe refactoring — Claude needs to know all call sites before changing a function signature.

Tool Chains: How Tools Work Together

The real power of tools emerges when they are chained together. No single tool solves a coding problem. It is the sequence of tool calls — decided by Claude's reasoning — that produces useful results. Here is a typical tool chain for fixing a bug:

Figure 11.4 — Tools chained together to fix a bug.

Each tool call is a separate API call where Claude decides what to invoke. The harness executes the tool, returns the result to Claude, and Claude decides the next step. This is the fundamental mechanism of tool use that you learned in earlier chapters — now applied at scale in a real product.

The key insight is that Claude decides the tool chain at runtime. There is no hard-coded sequence of "first grep, then read, then edit." Claude reasons about the task and chooses the right tools in the right order. If the tests pass on the first try, it skips the diagnostic step. If Grep finds nothing, it might try Glob instead. The chain is dynamic, not static.

Section 11.4

Context Management

Context is the most precious resource in an agentic system. The context window — the total amount of text Claude can process in a single conversation — is large but finite. Every file you read, every command output, every message back and forth consumes context. Managing it well is the difference between an agent that works reliably and one that loses track of what it is doing halfway through a task.

What Fills the Context Window

Every interaction adds to the context window. Here is what takes up space during a typical coding session:

Figure 11.5 — How the context window fills up during a coding session.

The exact proportions vary per session. A session with lots of file reading will be heavy on file contents. A session with many test runs will have more command output. The key insight is that context is always being consumed, and the harness must manage it actively to avoid running out of space before the task is complete.

CLAUDE.md — Persistent Memory Across Sessions

CLAUDE.md is a special markdown file that Claude Code reads at the start of every session. It lives in your project directory and contains project-specific instructions, conventions, and knowledge that persist across conversations. Think of it as a "readme for Claude" — telling the agent about your project's architecture, coding standards, and preferred workflows. 

# CLAUDE.md for my-project

## Project Structure
- src/         — Main source code (TypeScript)
- tests/       — Test files (Jest)
- scripts/     — Build and deployment scripts

## Coding Conventions
- Use functional components with hooks (no class components)
- All exported functions must have JSDoc comments
- Tests must cover edge cases, not just happy paths
- Use named exports, not default exports

## Common Commands
- npm test           — Run all tests
- npm run build      — Production build
- npm run lint       — Check for style issues
- npm run typecheck  — Verify TypeScript types

## Important Notes
- API keys are in .env (never commit this file)
- Database migrations must be backward-compatible
- The auth module uses JWTs with RS256 signing

This file is loaded into context at the beginning of each session, giving Claude instant knowledge about your project without needing to rediscover it every time. A well-written CLAUDE.md can dramatically improve Claude Code's effectiveness — it removes the cold-start problem where the agent has to explore the project from scratch.

Auto Memory — Learning Your Preferences

Beyond CLAUDE.md, Claude Code has an auto memory feature that learns your preferences across sessions. If you consistently correct Claude to use a certain coding style, prefer specific test patterns, or follow particular naming conventions, the system remembers these preferences and applies them automatically in future sessions.

Auto memory is stored at the user level, not the project level. This means your personal preferences follow you across all your projects, while CLAUDE.md stays project-specific. Together, they create a progressively more personalized and effective coding experience.

Compaction — What Happens When Context Fills Up

Every context window has a limit. As a session gets longer — more files read, more commands run, more messages exchanged — the context fills up. When it approaches the limit, Claude Code performs auto-compaction.

How Compaction Works

When context usage approaches the limit, Claude Code creates a condensed summary of the conversation: what the user originally asked, what has been done so far, which files have been changed and how, what the current state is, and what remains to do. The full verbose history is replaced with this compact summary, freeing up space for new work.

The user does not need to trigger this — it happens automatically and seamlessly. The session continues without interruption, but with a fresh budget of context space. Some detail from early in the conversation is lost, but the essential state is preserved.

Compaction is a trade-off: you lose fine-grained detail from earlier in the session, but you gain the ability to continue working on long, complex tasks. Without compaction, long sessions would simply fail when the context window fills up.

Skills — On-Demand Knowledge

Skills are reusable pieces of knowledge and workflow definitions that load into context only when needed. Instead of cramming every possible instruction into CLAUDE.md (which would waste context space), you can create skills that Claude loads on demand.

For example, you might have skills for:

  • "Deploy to production" — the exact sequence of commands, safety checks, and confirmations for your deployment process
  • "Add a new API endpoint" — the project's conventions for route definitions, validation, error handling, and tests
  • "Write a database migration" — the migration patterns your team uses, with templates and naming conventions

Skills keep CLAUDE.md lean and focused on general project information, while specialized knowledge loads only when relevant — conserving the precious context window.

Subagents — Fresh Context for Subtasks

When a task is large or complex, the main agent can spawn subagents — separate instances of Claude that each get their own fresh context window. This is perhaps the most powerful context management strategy.

Consider implementing a user dashboard feature that requires changes to the frontend (React components, CSS), the backend (API endpoints, validation), and the database (new tables, migrations). If the main agent does all of this in one context window, it will fill up quickly with file contents and command outputs from three different areas of the codebase.

Instead, the main agent can:

  1. Plan the overall approach and identify the subtasks
  2. Spawn a subagent for the frontend work (fresh context, only frontend files)
  3. Spawn a subagent for the backend work (fresh context, only backend files)
  4. Spawn a subagent for the database work (fresh context, only migration files)
  5. Collect the results and verify everything works together

Each subagent works in isolation with a clean context window, focused entirely on its subtask. This is both a context management strategy and a parallelism strategy — multiple subagents can work simultaneously.

Context Management Is the Hard Part

Building a coding agent is not just about connecting an LLM to tools. The real engineering challenge is managing context — deciding what the model sees, when it sees it, how to preserve essential information across long sessions, and when to delegate to subagents for fresh context. This is where most agentic systems succeed or fail.

Section 11.5

Permissions, Safety, and Checkpoints

Giving an AI agent access to your file system and shell is powerful — and potentially dangerous. A single wrong rm -rf or a misguided git push --force could cause real damage. Claude Code builds in multiple layers of safety to ensure the user always stays in control.

Checkpoints — Every Edit Is Reversible

Before Claude Code edits any file, it takes a checkpoint — a complete snapshot of the file's current state. If something goes wrong, or if you simply disagree with a change, you can instantly revert to any previous checkpoint. This is not like "undo" in an editor (which tracks individual keystrokes) — it is a full versioning system that tracks every change Claude makes during a session.

Figure 11.6 — The checkpoint system tracks every file change.

Checkpoints give you a safety net that makes it safe to let Claude experiment. If a refactoring attempt does not work out, you do not need to manually undo anything — just revert to the checkpoint before the change. This encourages bolder, more ambitious use of the agent.

Permission Modes

Claude Code offers three permission modes that control how much autonomy the agent has. You choose the mode based on how much you trust the agent for a particular task:

Mode File Edits Shell Commands Best For
Default Ask permission Ask permission Learning, sensitive codebases, first-time use
Auto-accept edits Auto-approved Ask for destructive ones Trusted projects, faster iteration
Plan mode Blocked entirely Read-only allowed Exploration, code review, planning

In Default mode, Claude asks before every edit and every potentially destructive command. You see every action before it happens. This is the safest option and the recommended starting point.

In Auto-accept edits mode, file edits are applied immediately without asking. Claude still asks permission for certain shell commands (like deleting files or installing packages), but the edit-verify cycle is much faster because you are not confirming every change.

In Plan mode, Claude cannot edit any files at all. It can only read code, run read-only commands, and explain what it would do. This is perfect for understanding a codebase, reviewing a proposed approach, or getting a plan before committing to changes.

Settings Hierarchy

Permissions and settings in Claude Code are organized in a hierarchy, from broadest to most specific. Higher levels can enforce rules that lower levels cannot override:

Figure 11.7 — Settings are scoped from organization-wide down to personal preferences.

This hierarchy ensures that an organization can enforce security policies — like "never run rm -rf /" or "always require approval for git push" — that individual users and projects cannot override. At the same time, individual users can customize their experience within the bounds set by the layers above.

Security Principles

Claude Code's security model is built on several core principles that work together:

  • Explicit consent — In default mode, every action requires user approval before it executes
  • Reversibility — Every file change can be undone via checkpoints, so mistakes are never permanent
  • Least privilege — Claude accesses only the files and commands relevant to the current task
  • Transparency — Every tool call and its result are visible to the user in the terminal output
  • Scoped access — Claude operates within the project directory, not your entire system
  • Graduated trust — You start with maximum safety and relax controls as you build confidence
Trust, But Verify

Even with these safety measures, the best practice is to review Claude's changes before considering them final. The checkpoint system makes this easy — you can see exactly what changed and roll back anything you disagree with. As you build trust with the agent over time, you can move to more autonomous permission modes for routine tasks.

Section 11.6

Extending Claude Code

Claude Code is not a closed system. It provides multiple extension points that let you customize its behavior, connect it to external services, and build more sophisticated workflows on top of its foundation. This extensibility is what makes it a platform for building agentic workflows, not just a tool.

Skills — Custom Workflows on Demand

A skill is a reusable piece of context — instructions, templates, or workflows — that Claude can load on demand. Unlike CLAUDE.md which is always loaded at session start, skills enter the context window only when they are relevant to the current task.

For example, you might create skills for:

  • "Deploy to production" — the exact steps, safety checks, smoke tests, and rollback procedures for your deployment pipeline
  • "Add a new API endpoint" — the project's conventions for route structure, request validation, error responses, and test coverage
  • "Write a database migration" — the migration patterns your team uses, with templates, naming conventions, and backward-compatibility rules
  • "Onboard a new service" — how to set up a new microservice with the team's standard boilerplate, CI/CD, and monitoring

Skills solve the tension between having rich context and conserving the context window. You can have dozens of skills covering every workflow your team uses, but only the relevant ones load into context for any given task.

MCP — Model Context Protocol

In Chapter 8, you learned about the Model Context Protocol (MCP) — the open standard for connecting AI models to external tools and data sources. Claude Code is a full MCP client, which means it can connect to any MCP server to gain new capabilities beyond its built-in tools.

Through MCP, Claude Code can:

  • Query databases directly — connect to Postgres, MySQL, or MongoDB and run queries
  • Interact with project management — read and update Jira tickets, Linear issues, GitHub Issues
  • Access internal documentation — search your company's wiki, Confluence, or Notion
  • Connect to custom APIs — any internal service your organization exposes via MCP
  • Integrate with monitoring — check error rates, read logs, inspect metrics from Datadog or similar

MCP transforms Claude Code from a coding assistant into a full engineering assistant that understands not just your code, but your entire development ecosystem — the tickets, the databases, the deployment pipelines, and the monitoring dashboards.

Hooks — Event-Driven Automation

Hooks let you attach custom behavior to specific events in Claude Code's lifecycle. They run automatically when certain events occur, regardless of what the model decides to do:

  • Before edit — Automatically format or lint code before Claude applies an edit
  • After edit — Run type checking, linting, or custom validation after every file change
  • Before command — Block certain dangerous commands or add safety checks
  • After session — Generate a summary of changes, create a pull request, or notify a Slack channel

Hooks are different from skills and CLAUDE.md instructions because they are enforced, not suggested. A skill tells Claude how to deploy; a hook automatically runs the linter after every edit whether Claude remembers to or not. This makes hooks ideal for guardrails and quality gates.

Subagents — Parallel Workers with Fresh Context

We introduced subagents in the context management section. Here, let us look at them as a powerful extension mechanism for tackling complex, multi-part tasks.

Figure 11.8 — Subagents work independently with their own context windows.

Key advantages of subagents:

  • Fresh context — each subagent starts with a clean context window, not polluted by unrelated files and outputs
  • Parallel execution — multiple subagents can work simultaneously on different parts of the task
  • Focused attention — each subagent sees only the files relevant to its subtask, leading to better results
  • Isolation — a subagent that takes a wrong turn does not corrupt the main agent's state or waste its context

Agent Teams (Experimental)

Taking subagents further, Agent Teams is an experimental feature where multiple Claude Code instances collaborate on a project simultaneously. Unlike subagents (which are spawned and managed by a main agent), agent teams are multiple independent agents working on different parts of the same repository, coordinating through shared state like git branches.

Imagine one agent implementing a feature on a branch, another writing tests on a separate branch, and a third updating documentation — all working in parallel. This is the frontier of multi-agent coding, where the bottleneck shifts from agent capability to coordination and conflict resolution.

The Edit-Test-Commit Loop

All of these features — tools, context management, permissions, and extensions — come together in what is often called the edit-test-commit loop. This is the fundamental rhythm of how Claude Code writes production-quality code:

  1. Edit — Make changes to the codebase using the Edit or Write tools
  2. Test — Run the test suite using Bash to verify correctness
  3. Fix — If tests fail, read the errors, understand the failure, adjust the code, and test again
  4. Commit — When all tests pass and types check, create a git commit with a meaningful message

This loop mirrors how experienced developers work. The agent does not just write code and hope for the best — it verifies its work, iterates on failures, and commits clean, tested changes. This is what separates a coding agent from a code generator: the agent takes responsibility for the quality of its output.

The Complete Architecture

Let us bring everything together in a final diagram that shows the complete Claude Code architecture — from user input at the top to external services at the bottom:

Figure 11.9 — The complete Claude Code architecture, from user input to external services.

Summary: The Five Pillars of a Coding Agent

Looking at Claude Code as a whole, we can extract the general principles for building any coding agent. Whether you use Claude Code or build your own, these five pillars remain the same:

The Five Pillars of a Coding Agent
  1. An agentic loop — The cycle of gathering context, taking action, and verifying results. This is the engine that drives autonomous behavior. Without it, you have a chatbot, not an agent.
  2. Rich tools — File operations, search, execution, web access, and code intelligence. Tools are what bridge the gap between reasoning and real-world impact. More tools mean more capabilities.
  3. Context management — Persistent memory (CLAUDE.md), auto-compaction, on-demand skills, and subagents with fresh context. Managing what the model sees is as important as the model itself.
  4. Safety and control — Checkpoints for reversibility, permission modes for graduated trust, settings hierarchies for organizational policy, and user interruption at every step.
  5. Extensibility — MCP for connecting to external services, hooks for automated guardrails, skills for custom workflows, and subagents for parallelism. A good agent is a platform that grows with its users.

These principles are not specific to Claude Code. Any coding agent — whether you build it from scratch or use an existing framework — will need to solve these same five challenges. The specific implementations differ (different tools, different context strategies, different permission models), but the architecture remains fundamentally the same.

What makes Claude Code remarkable is not any single one of these pillars — it is how they work together. The agentic loop uses tools to gather context. Context management decides what to load into the loop. Permissions constrain what the loop can do. Extensions expand what is possible. And checkpoints make the whole system safe to use aggressively. The system is greater than the sum of its parts.

In the chapters that follow, we will use these principles to guide our own agent-building efforts, starting with simpler use cases and working up toward full coding assistants. The foundation you have built in this chapter — understanding tool use, the agentic loop, context management, safety systems, and extensibility — gives you a complete mental model of how production coding agents work.

End of Chapter 11

Chapter 12

OpenClaw: The Modern Revolution

How an Austrian vibe coder's weekend side project became the fastest-growing open-source repository in history, ignited a global AI-agent craze, and launched the age of personal autonomous assistants — all while keeping your data on your own machine.

12.1 — The OpenClaw Story

An Austrian Vibe Coder

In the autumn of 2025, the AI landscape was dominated by massive corporate models and proprietary APIs. Building an agent that could autonomously handle everyday tasks — reading email, browsing the web, managing your calendar — still required stitching together dozens of services, APIs, and SDKs. It was powerful but complex, fragmented, and decidedly not private. Every request traveled to some distant data center; every response came back wrapped in metering and subscription logic.

Enter Peter Steinberger, an Austrian developer who described himself as a "vibe coder." Steinberger had earned a strong reputation in the Apple development community — he was well known for his work on iOS frameworks and developer tooling. But by late 2025, his obsession had shifted. He wanted a personal AI assistant that could actually do things: not just answer questions, but take actions in the real world. And he wanted it running entirely on his own machine, talking to him through the same messaging apps he already used every day. No cloud lock-in. No subscription treadmill. No data leaving his control. Just an open-source agent he could hack on, extend, and share with others.

The idea crystallized during a weekend hackathon. Steinberger had been experimenting with Anthropic's Claude model, impressed by its capacity for multi-step reasoning and tool use. He wondered: what if he could wrap Claude (or any capable LLM) in a lightweight local process that bridged his messaging platforms to the model's capabilities? The AI would receive messages from WhatsApp, Telegram, or Discord; reason about what the user wanted; execute the necessary actions (browsing, file editing, shell commands); and send the results back through the same messaging channel. All locally. All open-source.

Why "Vibe Coding"?

The term vibe coding describes a development style where the programmer gives high-level natural-language directions to an AI, then iterates by "vibes" — approving, rejecting, or steering the AI's code generation in real time. Rather than writing every line by hand, the vibe coder acts more like a creative director: setting intent, evaluating output, and course-correcting. Steinberger exemplified this workflow: much of OpenClaw was built by conversing with Claude and other models, blurring the line between author and tool. The result was a codebase that grew at extraordinary speed — a single developer producing what might have taken a small team months.

In November 2025, Steinberger published the first commit of a project he called Clawdbot — a playful nod to Anthropic's Claude model, which served as its default brain. The concept was deceptively simple: a single local process that bridges your messaging platforms (WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack, Signal, and iMessage) to AI coding agents, giving the AI the ability to browse the web, read and write files, execute shell commands, and control smart-home devices — all while keeping every byte of data on your own hardware.

The first version was rough. But it worked. And within days of the GitHub repository going public, developers around the world began starring, forking, and contributing. Something about the project had struck a nerve.

The Naming Saga

The project's journey to its current name is a story in itself — one that reveals how quickly open-source AI projects can collide with corporate trademarks, and how community identity can form around unexpected symbols.

November 2025 — "Clawdbot"
First public release on GitHub. The name plays on "Claude" + "bot" — a tongue-in-cheek acknowledgment that Anthropic's model was the project's default backend. Growth begins organically through Hacker News, Reddit, and word of mouth in developer communities. Stars accumulate rapidly.
January 27, 2026 — "Moltbot"
Anthropic's legal team files a trademark complaint, arguing that "Clawdbot" is too similar to "Claude" and could cause consumer confusion. Steinberger, characteristically pragmatic, renames the project within hours. The new name — Moltbot — references a lobster shedding its shell during a molt, a fitting metaphor for a project undergoing rapid transformation. The crustacean theme sticks.
January 30, 2026 — "OpenClaw"
Three days later, the community holds an informal poll and settles on a permanent name: OpenClaw. It keeps the crustacean motif ("claw") while signaling the project's open-source ethos ("Open"). The lobster mascot — claws cheerfully raised — becomes the official logo. The rename is seamless; GitHub redirects are set up, and the community barely misses a beat.
Figure 12.1 — The naming timeline: Clawdbot to Moltbot to OpenClaw, all within roughly two months of the project's birth.

Explosive Growth

What happened next stunned even seasoned open-source veterans. OpenClaw hit 247,000 GitHub stars and 47,700 forks by March 2, 2026 — barely four months after its first commit. To put that in perspective, it took most legendary open-source projects years to reach comparable numbers. Linux, the operating system kernel that runs the majority of the world's servers, accumulated its GitHub stars over decades. OpenClaw did it in a single winter.

247K
GitHub Stars
47.7K
Forks
4
Months to Reach
500+
Contributors

Several factors fueled this ascent:

  • Timing: The AI-agent wave was cresting. Developers were hungry for a unified, local-first agent framework. The market was full of cloud-only solutions that required expensive subscriptions and sent all data to remote servers. OpenClaw arrived at exactly the moment when frustration with that model peaked.
